SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/707a3e8a-ab58-4a2a-81db-2aebf10d1e34
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-of-directors
TITLE: 2015-2016 Strategic Goals
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

RSU5 Strategic Goals 2015-­‐2016
Focus on Student Achievement
Vision: RSU5 is committed to ensuring all children have a school experience characterized by
“personalized learning.” RSU5 embraces the responsibility to nurture the unique strengths
each student possesses while supporting every student to graduate ready for career, college
and citizenship.
Annual Goals:
• Review our continued implementation of strategic curriculum investments;
specifically, Teachers College Reading and Writing Workshop Model, Every Day
Math, Project Based Learning and Proficiency-­‐Based Learning.
• Strengthen Instructional Support by developing a framework to create consistency
and improvement. Encourage and support all schools to raise student aspirations
and develop systemic interventions to ensure learning progress for each student.
• Highlight stories of success and growth of students, teachers and community to
enhance pride in RSU5.
Focus on Teacher and Administrator Effectiveness
Vision: RSU5 employs teachers and administrators who value a strong collaborative culture
and shared responsibility for student learning. Teachers and administrators work in
professional learning communities (PLCs) to meet the needs of all learners and engage in on
going, job-­‐embedded professional learning.
Annual Goals:
• Monitor implementation of Year #2 of Teacher Professional Growth and Evaluation
Pilot and Year #1 of Administrator Professional Growth and Evaluation Pilot, and
establish a coherent system of teacher and administrator growth and evaluation for
2016-­‐2017.
• Select and pilot a comprehensive superintendent performance evaluation.
• Assess efforts to get to scale across RSU5 with Professional Learning Communities
by setting SMART goals and reviewing the results.
• Re-­‐establish an RSU5 Professional Development Committee to provide more
opportunities for teacher input and set the context for more support for initiatives.
Focus on Stewardship of Resources.
Vision: RSU5 takes seriously its responsibility to be good stewards of resources. Thorough
financial planning for strategic priorities, proactive facilities management and sustainability
planning are the hallmarks of a high performing district.
Annual Goals:
• Align the FY 17 district budget with strategic priorities; adjust the budget timeline
to provide time to address educational as well as financial impacts and to allow
plenty of discussion and communication with stakeholder groups.
• Monitor high school addition and renovation project.
• Develop a long-­‐range plan for capital investments, technology and education
initiatives that are aligned with district goals.
• Explore ways to create efficiencies working in concert with the three towns.

Focus on Accountability
Vision: RSU5 will communicate with all stakeholders regularly about the schools and will
sustain efforts to publish performance indicators to demonstrate progress in the focus areas of
Student Achievement, Teacher Effectiveness and Stewardship of Resources.
Annual Goals:
• Monitor, on a regular basis, progress on yearly goals.
• Develop specific strategies for improving communications about RSU5, including
updating the district's website on a regular basis and in a timely fashion, and more
frequent newsletters and submissions to local publications. Increase opportunities
to engage the public in conversations about school related issues and open up
avenues for public stakeholder feedback and input.
• Continue RSU5 Leadership Committee meetings with leaders from the three towns.
• Create a district-­‐wide, consistent process for accurately tracking migration patterns
in and out of RSU5.
Approved by Board: 10/14/15

----------------------------------------

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/60abb835-c8ce-4244-a283-011dd94f3817
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-of-directors
TITLE: 2014-2015 Board of Directors Interim Goals
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

RSU5 Board of Directors
Suggested Interim Goals
1. Support enthusiastically and market effectively an FY’16 Budget that provides the resources
necessary to improve the overall performance of RSU5 schools.
2. Establish and organize a Leadership Council comprised of representatives from each of the
three RSU5 communities.
3. Begin an ongoing, and organized process to blur the lines among the RSU5 communities in
order to ensure a true regional approach to the education of our students.
4. Improve communication between the RSU5 Board of Directors and its various publics.
Discussed: 1/14/15
“Is it good for kids?”
Regional School Unit No. 5
17 West St., Freeport, ME 04032 Telephone: 865-0928x5 E-mail: mcmanusg@rsu5.org

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/9443ec73-b849-49e0-b5cb-d85b00e10264
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-of-directors
TITLE: 2013-2014 Board of Directors Goals
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

RSU5 BOARD OF DIRECTORS
GOALS for 2013-2014
The RSU 5 Board of Directors has adopted a Strategic Framework that outlines detailed
academic program and financial goals. These were adopted with the intent of enhancing
the quality of learning for our students. Indicators for each goal area were identified as
was a communication plan for keeping the public informed about our progress toward
attaining these goals.
In addition to those district-level goals, the Board sets goals for its own development.
Annually, the Board completes a self-evaluation based on dimensions of Board
competency. The results are used to establish goals and planning for the year. The
following goals were established for 2013-2014:
I. Goal Area: EFFECTIVE COMMUNICATION
To effectively communicate with parents and the larger community so that they
understand and engage in actively supporting the education of our students.
 The Board will host a Fall meeting with governance members from each community in
an effort to increase communications and build positive relations. (Board Chair and
administration responsibility
 The Board will hold at least 1 workshop meeting in each town on an annual basis.
Members of the public will be invited to attend an informal social/coffee in each town
to provide an opportunity for the public to speak individually with members of the
Board. That component of the Board meeting will not be videotaped for broadcast
purposes. (Board Chair and administration responsibility)
 The Board will hold a second meeting in each town annually during the budget process
and extend invitations to each town’s governance officials. The goal is to increase
communication with town officials while increasing the public’s knowledge and
awareness of components of the draft 2014-2015 budget prior to Board adoption.
(Board Chair and administration responsibility)
 A Board member will ask for an RSU5 Communication to be added to the governing
body’s agenda twice over the course of the year. The goal is to provide the town’s
governing body with and update on current RSU5 events. (Board and administration
responsibility)
 RSU5 will extend an invitation to the Chair and Manager/Assistant of each town’s
governing board to create a Leadership Team with the RSU5 Board Chair and
Superintendent. The purpose of the meetings is to discuss issues of importance to the
three towns and the RSU. (Board and administration responsibility)
 Board members will summarize the process and information generated before asking
the Board to take a final vote on decisions. The goal is to better educate the public
about the process leading up to a final vote. (Board and administration responsibility)
 Board members will effectively communicate the educational benefits of the RSU.
The goal is to create one large RSU community across all three towns. (Board and
administration responsibility)
 Good News and Recognition presentations will be provided by teachers/administrators
about what is going on in each school. (Administration responsibility)

II. Goal Area: FREEPORT HIGH SCHOOL FACILITY
To research and consider action on the most effective methods of addressing
overcrowding, facility, and program needs at the Freeport High School.
 If the November referendum question(s) are approved, charge a Building Committee
representative of all stakeholders to provide direction and advice to the project’s
construction team for Design and development of Construction Documents for
renovation and addition(s) to the Freeport High School facility, fields, and grounds.
(Board and administration responsibility)
 If the November referendum question(s) are not approved, direct administration to
develop a 3-5 year plan to address overcrowding, facility, and program needs at the
Freeport High School. (Administration responsibility)
III. Goal Area: FINANCIAL
To provide leadership for securing support for the district budget with a specific plan
for explaining and defending the budget to the public.
 The Board will be informed about programs and needs through administrative reports
and workshops during the months prior to beginning the public budget development
process. Citizens will be invited and encouraged to attend these sessions. (Board,
administration, and public responsibility)
 The Board will establish budget priorities prior to the public budget development
process. These will be both proactive with a focus on implementation of the Strategic
Framework and reactive to current conditions. Citizens will be invited to provide input
to help the Board understand the public’s priorities. (Board, administration, and public
responsibility)
 The Board will educate themselves and the public about how costs are shared between
the three communities as a result of the Essential Programs and Services (EPS)
distribution of state subsidy and the RSU5 Cost Sharing Agreement. (Board, Finance
Committee, and administration responsibility)
 The Board will meet with the Selectmen or Town Council and Budget Advisory
Committee members from each of our three communities to discuss budget items and
tax impact to citizens and to gain their input on the proposed budget. (Finance
Committee and administration responsibility)
 The Board will consider the most effective way of getting the greatest number of
people to attend the Annual Budget Meeting. The Board will also explore the
possibility of holding the Annual Budget Meeting in Durham with a decision in
January, 2014 (Finance Committee and administration responsibility)
 Structure the Board’s budget meetings so the Board has time for deliberations prior to a
final vote on the budget. (Board Chair and administration responsibility)

IV. Goal Area: NEGOTIATIONS
To work effectively with the Coastal Education Association (CEA) to successfully
resolve educator and support professionals collective bargaining negotiations.
 The Board will use data to identify issues and share that data with the Association.
(Board and administration responsibility)
 The Board will respectfully consider all issues raised through the negotiations process.
(Board and administration responsibility)
 The Board will work to resolve issues in the best interests of students while balancing
the needs of both the CEA and the community. (Board and administration
responsibility)
Adopted: 10/23/13

----------------------------------------

TITLE: 2012-2013 Board of Directors Goals
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF

RSU5 BOARD OF DIRECTORS
GOALS for 2012-2013
The RSU 5 Board of Directors has adopted a Strategic Framework that outlines detailed
academic program and financial goals. These were adopted in February 2010 with the
intent of enhancing the quality of learning for our students. Indicators for each goal area
were identified as was a communication plan for keeping the public informed about our
progress toward attaining these goals.
In addition to those district-level goals, the Board sets goals for its own development.
Annually, the Board completes a self-evaluation based on dimensions of Board
competency. The results are used to establish goals and planning for the year. The
following goals were established for 2012-2013:
I. Goal Area: EFFECTIVE COMMUNICATION
To effectively communicate with parents and the larger community so that they
understand and engage in actively supporting the education of our students.
 The Board will host a Fall meeting with governance members from each community in
an effort to increase communications and build positive relations. (Board Chair and
administration responsibility
 The Board will hold a workshop meeting in each town on an annual basis. Members of
the public will be invited to attend an informal social/coffee in each town prior to the
workshop to provide an opportunity for the public to speak with members of the Board.
That component of the Board meeting will not be videotaped for broadcast purposes.
(Board Chair and administration responsibility)
 The Board will hold a second meeting in each town annually during the budget
process. The goal is to increase the public’s knowledge and awareness of components
of the draft 2013-2014 budget prior to Board adoption. (Board Chair and
administration responsibility)
 Board members will summarize the process and information generated before asking
the Board to take a final vote on decisions. The goal is to better educate the public
about the process leading up to a final vote. (Board and administration responsibility)
 Board members will effectively communicate the educational benefits of the RSU.
The goal is to create one large RSU community across all three towns. (Board and
administration responsibility)
 Provide Good News and Recognition presentations by teachers/administrators about
what is going on in each school. (Administration responsibility)
 The Board’s Negotiations Team will consider ways to build stronger working
relationships with teachers. (Negotiations Team and administration responsibility)

II. Goal Area: FREEPORT HIGH SCHOOL FACILITY
To research and consider action on the most effective methods of addressing
overcrowding, facility, and program needs at the Freeport High School.
 Consider approval of the expenditure of funds for Concept Design and related budget
to address the needs at the high school. (Board and administration responsibility)
 Consider development of an Advisory Committee representative of all stakeholders to
provide direction and advice to the project’s design team for development of Concept
Design and budget for renovation and addition(s) to the Freeport High School facility,
fields, and grounds. (Board and administration responsibility)
 Consider action on a possible referendum vote regarding the Freeport High School
facility, fields, and grounds. (Board and administration responsibility)
 If the Board supports a referendum vote, consider action to support educational efforts
about the project. (Board, administration, and Advisory Committee responsibility)
III. Goal Area: FINANCIAL
To provide leadership for securing support for the district budget with a specific plan
for explaining and defending the budget to the public.
 The Board will be informed about programs and needs through administrative reports
and workshops during the months prior to beginning the public budget development
process. Citizens will be invited and encouraged to attend these sessions. (Board,
administration, and public responsibility)
 The Board will establish budget priorities prior to the public budget development
process. These will be both proactive with a focus on implementation of the Strategic
Framework and reactive to current conditions. Citizens will be invited to provide input
to help the Board understand the public’s priorities. (Board, administration, and public
responsibility)
 The Board will meet with the Selectmen or Town Council and Budget Advisory
Committee members from each of our three communities to discuss budget items and
tax impact to citizens and to gain their input on the proposed budget.(Finance
Committee and administration responsibility)
 Consider the most effective way of getting the greatest number of people to attend the
Annual Budget Meeting. (Finance Committee and administration responsibility)
 Structure the Board’s budget meetings so the Board has time for deliberations prior to a
final vote on the budget. (Board Chair and administration responsibility)
Final 9/26/12

TITLE: 2011-2012 Board of Directors Goals
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

RSU5 BOARD OF DIRECTORS
GOALS for 2011-2012
The RSU 5 Board of Directors has adopted a Strategic Framework that outlines detailed
academic program and financial goals. These were adopted in February 2010 with the
intent of enhancing the quality of learning for our students. Indicators for each goal area
were identified as was a communication plan for keeping the public informed about our
progress toward attaining these goals.
In addition to those district-level goals, the Board sets goals for its own development.
Annually, the Board completes a self-evaluation based on dimensions of Board
competency. The results are used to establish goals and planning for the year. The
following goals were established for 2011-2012:
I. Goal Area: EFFECTIVE COMMUNICATION
To effectively communicate with parents and the larger community so that they
understand and engage in actively supporting the education of our students.
 Communications sub-committee members will contact parent groups at each school
and municipal officers from each town at the beginning of the school year and
regarding specific issues throughout the year, as needed. (Communications sub-
committee responsibility)
 The Board will hold a workshop meeting in each town on an annual basis. Members of
the public will be invited to attend an informal social/coffee in each town prior to the
workshop to provide an opportunity for the public to speak with members of the Board.
That component of the Board meeting will not be videotaped for broadcast purposes.
(Board Chair and administration responsibility)
 The Board will hold a second meeting in each town annually during the budget
process. The goal is to increase the public’s knowledge and awareness of components
of the draft 2012-2013 budget prior to Board adoption. (Board Chair and
administration responsibility)
 Board members will summarize the process and information generated before asking
the Board to take a final vote on decisions. The goal is to better educate the public
about the process leading up to a final vote. (Board and administration responsibility)
 Board members will effectively communicate the educational benefits of the RSU.
The goal is to create one large RSU community across all three towns. (Board and
administration responsibility)

II. Goal Area: BOARD DEVELOPMENT
To provide appropriate Board training and support opportunities for members to
“develop and reinforce the skills required for effective policy-making, budget
planning, and communications.” (Policy BIB)
 New Board members will be provided an orientation meeting and assigned a mentor
for support and informational purposes. (Board, Chair, and administration
responsibility)
 Board members will be encouraged to attend the Maine School Board Association
annual conference. (Board and administration responsibility)
 Efforts will be made to move to common municipal election dates for RSU Board
elections. The Communications Committee will approach the Selectmen or Town
Council from each of our three communities with a request for either a June or
November election date. The goal is to bring new Board members on at the same time
for more effective training and development. (Communications sub-committee
responsibility)
III. Goal Area: FINANCIAL
To provide leadership for securing support for the district budget with a specific plan
for explaining and defending the budget to the public.
 The Board will be informed about programs and needs through administrative reports
and workshops during the months prior to beginning the public budget development
process. Citizens will be invited and encouraged to attend these sessions. (Board,
administration, and public responsibility)
 The Board will establish budget priorities prior to the public budget development
process. These will be both proactive with a focus on implementation of the Strategic
Framework and reactive to current conditions. Citizens will be invited to provide input
to help the Board understand the public’s priorities. (Board, administration, and public
responsibility)
 The Board will meet with the Selectmen or Town Council from each of our three
communities to discuss budget priorities and educate municipal officers about the
financial health of the RSU. (Board Chair and administration responsibility)
Board of Directors Adopted: 9/28/11

SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-of-directors
TITLE: 2010-2011 Board of Directors Goals
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

RSU5 BOARD OF DIRECTORS
GOALS for 2010-2011
The RSU 5 Board of Directors has adopted a Strategic Framework that outlines detailed
academic program and financial goals. These were adopted in February 2010 with the
intent of enhancing the quality of learning for our students. Indicators for each goal area
were identified as was a communication plan for keeping the public informed about our
progress toward attaining these goals.
In addition to those district-level goals, the Board sets goals for its own development.
Annually, the Board completes a self-evaluation based on dimensions of Board
competency. The results are used to establish goals and planning for the year. The
following goals were established for 2010-2011:
I. Goal Area: EFFECTIVE COMMUNICATION
To effectively communicate with parents and the larger community so that they
understand and engage in actively supporting the education of our students.
 Communications sub-committee members will contact parent groups at each school
and municipal officers from each town on a regular basis throughout the year.
(Communications sub-committee responsibility)
 The Board will hold a workshop meeting in each town on an annual basis. Members of
the public will be invited to attend an informal social/coffee in each town prior to the
workshop to provide an opportunity for the public to speak with members of the Board.
That component of the Board meeting will not be videotaped for broadcast purposes.
(Board Chair and administration responsibility)
II. Goal Area: BOARD DEVELOPMENT
To provide appropriate Board training and support opportunities for members to
“develop and reinforce the skills required for effective policy-making, budget
planning, and communications.” (Policy BIB)
 New Board members will be provided an orientation meeting and assigned a mentor
for support and informational purposes. (Board, Chair, and administration
responsibility)
 Board members will be encouraged to attend the Maine School Board Association
annual conference. (Board and administration responsibility)
 Additional opportunities for Board development will be considered to share best
practices and converse with members from other boards. (Board and administration
responsibility)

III. Goal Area: FINANCIAL
To provide leadership for securing support for the district budget with a specific plan
for explaining and defending the budget to the public.
 The Board will be informed about programs and needs through administrative reports
and workshops during the months prior to beginning the public budget development
process. Citizens will be invited and encouraged to attend these sessions. (Board,
administration, and public responsibility)
 The Board will establish budget priorities prior to the public budget development
process. These will be both proactive with a focus on implementation of the Strategic
Framework and reactive to current conditions. Citizens will be invited to provide input
to help the Board understand the public’s priorities. (Board, administration, and public
responsibility)
 Board members will be knowledgeable about all areas of the budget but will become
“expert” in one assigned Article. The “Budget Advocate” for each Article will take
primary responsibility for responding to questions and explaining Board decision
making regarding that area of the budget. (Board and administration responsibility)
Board of Directors Adopted: 9/22/10

----------------------------------------

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/8c7fb915-5d8e-4878-9703-906988abcf7b
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/standing-committees/facilities-and-operations
TITLE: February 11, 2026 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Tom Gray, Superintendent of Schools Erica Mazzeo, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources Elisha Morris, Director of Instructional Support
Facilities and Operations Committee
Wednesday, February 11, 2026
5:00 p.m. - Freeport High School - Library
Minutes
Committee Members Present: Michelle Ritcheson, Kelly Sink, Karen Breer
Committee Members Absent: None
Administrators in Attendance: Tom Gray, Superintendent; Glen Reynolds, Director of Facilities; Kelly
Wentworth, Director of Finance and Human Resources
Guest: Molly Meyer, FHS Tennis Coach
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Meeting called to order at 5:00pm
1. FHS Tennis courts update- Director Reynolds updated the committee that the contract
for design services was signed and CHA has scheduled the site survey to be completed on
2/18/26. Director Reynolds indicated that the core sampling has not yet been scheduled.
2. Energy Audit Phase 1 project update- Director Reynolds updated the committee that the
lighting project has been completed at Pownal Elementary and the District Office.
Director Rerynolds shared that the lighting vendor is currently working at Freepoort
Middle School. The next location for this project is Mast Landing School.
3. DCS automation replacement- Director Reynolds updated the committee that the
contract has been signed and material is being ordered. EMC is the vendor who bid the
project, and Maine Controls was the winning bidder.
4. FHS baseball dugout roof replacement donated by Boosters- Director Reynolds updated
the committee that the baseball boosters have asked if they can replace the two roofs on
the baseball dugouts at Freeport High School. The estimated cost for this replacement is
around $3,000.00 dollars. Director Reyolds shared that new roofs will be the same as the
current ones on the softball dugouts as they are metal. The boosters would like to begin
the replacement in mid March before baseball season.

5. Guest: Molly Meyer FHS Tennis Coach shared some information regarding the applying
for a USTA grant to help offset the cost of installing new tennis courts. Molly provided the
committee with a document showing what information would be needed for approval of
the grant. Molly also shared that the grant for indoor courts would be estimated at
$35,000 per court and $25,000 per court for outdoor courts. The maximum grant
amount awarded is $150,000.

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Tom Gray, Superintendent of Schools Erica Mazzeo, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources Elisha Morris, Director of Instructional Support
Facilities and Operations Committee
Wednesday, January 14, 2026
5:00 p.m. - Freeport High School - Library
Minutes
Committee Members Present: Michelle Ritcheson, Kelly Sink, Karen Breer
Committee Members Absent:
Administrators in Attendance: Tom Gray, Superintendent; Glen Reynolds, Director of Facilities;
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance and Human Resources
Guest: Molly Meyer FHS Tennis Coach
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Meeting called to order at 5:03pm
1. Update FHS Tennis Courts - Director Reynolds reported that CHA was the only respondent to
the engineering and design services RFP. Director Reynolds reported that RSU5
Administration had met with CHA regarding the scope of work. CHA submitted a proposal for
design services that included both replacement of the three existing asphalt courts as well as
the addition of a fourth court, if feasible, with post-tension concrete construction. Director
Reynolds shared with the Committee that in his research Post- Tension concrete provides the
overall best results in both durability, longevity, and overall up-keep. A few committee members
and Molly expressed concerns with just getting pricing on the post-tension concrete option. It
was decided that we would ask the vendor to also include pricing for asphalt courts.
2. Update on the Energy Audit Phase 1 Project - Director Reynolds shared with the Committee
that the lighting project has officially begun at Pownal Elementary School. Director Reynolds
also shared that a new fire panel was installed at Pownal Elementary as well as the additional
fire protection devices were added at Durham Community School.
3. Update on Capital Project list - Director Reynolds shared that over December break the boys
bathroom floor was replaced at Morse Street School.
Motion to adjourn was made by Michelle Ritcheson second by Kelly Sink at 5:43 p.m.
Next meeting dates:
February 11, 2026, March 11, 2026, April 8, 2026, May 6, 2026, June 3, 2026

Joint Facilities and Operations Committee & Finance Committee Minutes
Wednesday, December 10, 2025
5:00 p.m. - Freeport High School Library
Committee Members Present: Michelle Ritcheson, Kelly Sink, Christopher Savaiano
Committee Members Absent: Karen Breer
Administrators: Superintendent Tom Gray, Director of Facilities & Grounds Glen Reynolds, & Director of
Finance & HR Kelly Wentworth
Minutes
Meeting called to order at 5:06 P.M.
1. Capital Planning & Financing Discussion
a. Energy Audit - Phase 2-
b. Vehicle & Equipment Replacement Plan - Phase 2
i. The committees reviewed the listing of the Energy Audit Phase II projects
and the FY27 vehicles and equipment proposed for replacement. The joint
committee then reviewed the updated financial planning tool, showing the
use of $152,364 while also adding $309,466 to the taxation portion of FY27
budget.
ii. The committee was in support of moving forward with Phase 2. The
amounts will move into the FY27 budget proposal and will be reviewed by
the full Board of Directors.
c. FHS Tennis Courts Replacement
i. Director Reynolds & Director Wentworth reported that the FHS Tennis
Court Replacement RFP was sent out. The Superintendent, Director of
Facilities & Grounds, and the Director of Finance & HR met November 24th
to review the proposals. There was one respondent, CHA Architects. A
representative from CHA will be in the district to review the scope of work
and to discuss schedule and pricing on December 18th.
2. Other
a. FHS Pumps- Director Reynolds explained that the variable flow devices in the two stage
pumps that run the heat in the Freeport Performing Arts (FPAC) area at Freeport High
School are failing and need to be replaced. Director Wentworth identified the Capital
17 West Street Freeport, ME 04032 · 207-865-0928

Reserve Account as a possible funding source. Michelle Ritcheson made a motion,
seconded by Kelly Sink to approve up to $30,000 from Capital Reserves for the variable
flow device replacement in the two stage pumps in the FPAC area at Freeport High School.
All members present voted in favor. The Board of Directors will need to approve this
request at their next meeting.
b. FY25 Audit Update- Audit continues to progress. Director Wentworth had just replied to
the Auditor with a few clarifications.
c. Facilities Energy Audit Lighting Upgrade Update- 100% of the materials and fixtures are
in for the Central Office and 95% are in for Pownal Elementary School. Work is scheduled
to begin early winter.
d. Next meeting of the Facilities and Operations Committee will be January 14, 2026 @ FHS
and the next meeting of the Finance Committee will be January 28, 2026 @ FHS
Meeting adjourned at 6:02 P.M.

Minutes
Committee Members Present: Kelly Sink, Malik Farlow, Michelle Ritcheson
Committee Members Absent:
Administrators: Jean Skorapa, Glen Reynolds, Kelly Wentworth
1. RFP Update
a. Pownal Elementary School Septic
1. The district received six proposals.
1. CCC Construction LLC - $179,755
2. Copp Excavating, Inc - $213, 904
3. R.N. Willey & Sons Excavating - $163,709.86
4. Steadfast Earthworks - $221,800
5. A+ Septic, Inc. - $284,981
6. Bursey Enterprises - $162,682
2. The following criteria was used to select the proposal deemed to provide
the best value for RSU5:
1. Proposer Experience and Customer Service History - 50%
2. Availability for installations and completion date deadline - 40%
3. Durability/Warranty & Ease of Maintenance - 10%
3. Copp Excavating, Inc. was selected for the project.
b. Pownal Elementary School Playground Equipment
1. The district received two proposals.
1. Maine Recreation & Design - $50,330
2. Ben Schaffer Recreation - $90,040.25
2. The following criteria was used to select the proposal deemed to provide
the best value for RSU5:
1. Proposer Experience and Customer Service History - 50%
17 West Street Freeport, ME 04032 · 207-865-0928

2. Availability for installations and completion date deadline - 40%
3. Durability/Warranty & Ease of Maintenance - 10%
3. Maine Recreation & Design was selected for the project.
2. Energy Audit Phase One Project Update - Director Reynolds informed the committee
the projects have not yet gone out to bid. He met with EMC to develop a tentative
schedule for the projects that is least intrusive to summer programming. The schedule
will be finalized once vendors are selected.
a. Freeport High School - Boiler Replacement
b. LED Lighting Upgrade

SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/standing-committees/facilities-and-operations
TITLE: January 10, 2024 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

“Toinspireandsupporteverylearnerbychallengingminds,buildingcharacter,sparkingcreativity,andnurturingpassions.”
JeanSkorapa,SuperintendentofSchools CynthiaAlexander,AssistantSuperintendentofSchools
KellyWentworth,DirectorofFinance&HumanResources JuneSellers,Ed.D.,DirectorofInstructionalSupport
Facilities and Operations Committee
Wednesday, January 10, 2024
5:00 p.m. - Freeport High School Library
Minutes
Committee Members Present: Malik Farlow, Michelle Ritcheson
Committee Members Absent: Kelly Sink
Administrators: Jean Skorapa, Glen Reynolds
Agenda Items and Discussion:
1. Energy Audit Update
a. Phase l of the energy audit is well underway. Lighting was completed last week.
The outside envelope and ventilation audit is in process. Energy Management
Consultants (EMC) will provide a written report within 60 days.
2. Analysis of Capital Projects Funding
a. The district used COVID relief funding for several projects during the last few
years in addition to local funding.
b. During the prior three budget cycles the district used close to the entire $400,000
that was allocated in addition to the COVID funds.
3. Capital Improvement Plan
a. 2023-2024 Items Addressed
1. District-wide: Shades
2. Freeport Middle School: Aluminum Doors
3. Morse Street School Office Carpet (Tripping Hazard)
4. Central Office Carpet (Roof Leak Damage)
5. Other items on hold while septic system at Pownal Elementary School is
being evaluated
b. 2024-2025 Budget Requests
1. Items will be presented to the board during the budget process ($445,000
proposed)
a. Freeport High School
1. Gym Roof - $200,000
17WestStreetFreeport,ME04032· 207-865-0928

2. 3 Additional Single Stall Bathrooms - $35,000
3. Freeport Performing Arts Painting/Carpet - $55,500
b. Freeport Middle School
1. 3 Additional Single Stall Bathrooms - $25,000
2. Parking Lot Extension - $60,000
c. Pownal Elementary School
1. Plumbing and Abatement - $55,000
d. Durham Community School
1. Modular Classroom Ceiling - $15,000
e. There are other items that should be completed but are not
included in the budget.
2. The committee discussed the need to increase the amount allocated for
capital projects as the budgeted amount has remained the same for many
years.
c. An articulated multi-year plan will be further developed when the energy audit
has been completed.
4. Future Agenda Items
a. Energy Audit Report
5. Meeting Dates
February 14, 2024
March 13, 2024
April 10, 2024
May 8, 2024
June 5, 2024

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/7b6c6fd7-7bb0-46e0-a6d7-31279cf9f44a
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/standing-committees/facilities-and-operations-clone
TITLE: January 9, 2025 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Jean Skorapa, Superintendent of Schools Cynthia Alexander, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources June Sellers, Ed.D., Director of Instructional Support
Stipend Committee
January 9, 2025
Meeting held via Zoom
Minutes
Committee Members in Attendance: Kara Kaikini, Elisabeth Munsen, Lisa Blier, Lynn Shea
Guests: Emily Grimm, Charlie Mellon
Absent: None
Submitted By: Jean Skorapa, Superintendent
Agenda Items and Discussion:
1. Review of New Stipend Request - MLS Gaming Club
Principal Grimm attended the meeting and explained the rationale for the need for the MLS
Gaming Club stipend position. The weekly gaming club run by a teacher volunteer is extremely
popular with students and is well attended. The funding for the Gaming Club will come from
one unfilled Drama Club position.
The committee unanimously approved the MLS Gaming Club stipend as proposed. The stipend
will be presented to the RSU 5 Board of Directors for approval.
2. Review of New Stipend Request - FHS Senior Project Coordinator (3rd position)
Assistant Principal Mellon attended the meeting and explained the rationale for the need for a
third FHS Senior Project Coordinator. Currently there are two Senior Project Coordinators. Due
to the growth of the program, there is a need for a third stipend position. The funding for the
third FHS Senior Project Coordinator will come from the unfilled Fishing Club position.
The committee unanimously approved the FHS Senior Project Coordinator (3rd position)
stipend as proposed. The stipend will be presented to the RSU 5 Board of Directors for
approval.
17 West Street Freeport, ME 04032 · 207-865-0928

TITLE: January 11, 2019 Agenda

RSU5 Strategic Planning Advisory Committee
Agenda
January 11, 2019
7:30 a.m. – 9:00 a.m.
1. Continue revision of the following Mission/Vision statement
DRAFT
RSU 5 Mission Statement:
To develop….
To engage the imagination…
To empower…..
Self-governance
that challenges minds, engages creativity, nurtures passions, and inspires community
involvement.
Empathetic, kind respectful to others confident motivated goal-oriented independent critical
thinker problem solver interpersonal skills works well with others
Students graduate as compassionate, honest, respectful and responsible citizens who continue to
learn and contribute to their communities for life.
RSU 5 Vision:
Students:
In RSU5, we empower learners to become healthy, compassionate and responsible citizens who
contribute to their local and global communities. Our students are creative problem solvers and
critical thinkers who communicate effectively in an evolving world.
Learning:
RSU5 responds to individual students’ needs and prepares students to thrive in their personal and
professional lives. Learning is personalized to challenge students and to explore their passions.

Students choose from a variety of diverse and engaging experiences, including opportunities for
active, experiential, real-world learning.
Culture/Community:
In RSU5, students learn in joyful environments that are physically and emotionally safe. (???)
Our school culture fosters well-being, respect, and curiosity while challenging students with
clearly defined and rigorous expectations. Partnerships involving parents and community inspire
and support enriched learning for all students.
Students graduate as compassionate, honest, respectful and responsible citizens who continue to
learn and contribute to their communities for life.

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/a9475d9d-ce2b-418e-8822-941a43a974b6
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/advisory-committees/fhs-building-advisory-committee-archive
TITLE: here
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

RSU No. 5 Board of Directors
Freeport High School Building Advisory Committee
Committee Charge:
Whereas the RSU5 Board of Directors recognizes the importance of safe, suitable facilities to
support and enhance a world-class educational system, we hereby charge the Freeport High
School Building Advisory Committee (FHSBAC) to oversee a building project to construct and
equip addition(s), renovations, and improvements to the Freeport High School building, grounds,
and facilities as authorized by the voters on November 5, 2013.
The FHSBAC will reference a multitude of documents as resource materials to influence their
decision-making. These include but are not limited to the following:
! Previous facilities reports including the Master Plan, Fields Study, and Concept
work done prior to the referendum vote;
! FHS Visioning discussions and documents; and
! Other related materials.
Committee Membership:
The FHSBAC will be comprised of 23 stakeholders representing staff and the community. The
committee will be comprised of representatives from the three towns and the following
stakeholder groups.
! Teachers (3)
! Administrators (3) FHS Principal, Athletic Director, Director of Facilities
! Parents/Community members (9)
! Students (3)
! Board of Directors Facility Subcommittee members (1)
! Board of Directors representative (1)
! Superintendent of Schools (1), ex officio
! Director of Finance (1)
! Former Chair of the Freeport High School Renovation Advisory Committee (1)
Decision-Making Considerations:
The FHSBAC will strive to build consensus among committee members when making
recommendations to the RSU5 Board of Directors. Final authority for all decisions relating to
the project rests with the RSU5 Board of Directors.
Meetings:
Monthly meetings of the FHSBAC will be held on the first Wednesday of each month from 8:00 a.m.
to 9:00 a.m. at Freeport High School. Additional meetings may be scheduled as needed. An
organizational meeting and election of a FHSBAC chair will be held on January 7, 2015.
Individuals who wish to be considered for membership on the FHSBAC should submit their names to
William J. Michaud, Interim Superintendent of Schools, before December 5, 2014.
The RSU5 Board will appoint individuals to the FHSBAC at the December 10, 2014 Board of
Directors’ meeting
Adopted by Board of Directors: 11/19/14
Revised: 1/15/15

----------------------------------------

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
April 11, 2018 at 7:30 a.m.
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, John Simoneau/Committee Chairman Becky Foley, Superintendent/
RSU 5, Jen Gulko, Principal/FHS, Dennis Ouelette/Owner’s Rep., David Smail/FHS,
Michelle Lickteig/RSU 5 Business Office, Kim Lamarre, FHS Building Committee Member/
Kevin Nadeau, FHS Building Committee Member/Tim Giddinge, FHS Building Committee
Member/Michelle Peacock, FHS Building Committee Member and Julie Coleman, FHS
Building Committee Member
Purpose: Monthly Building Committee Meeting
These notes were prepared by Lyndon Keck to the best of his ability. Please notify PDT Architects within seventy-
two (72) hours with any corrections or changes.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. John Simoneau opened the meeting at 7:35 a.m.
2. John presented an overview of work that had been completed. He noted the
punchlist was finished over the Christmas holidays. It was also noted we are
now in the one-year warranty period. Several items mentioned will need to
be reviewed for warranty repairs. One included the stair treads at the
monumental stair. Another was the countertops at the Food Court.
3. John presented an overview of items included on Change Order #24 which
resulted in an overall credit of $24,155.90 to RSU 5.
4. John Simoneau reviewed items on Change Order #25 which involved the
final resolution of ledge at the project. This included an overall additional
amount of approximately $8,641.50.
5. There was discussion about withholding $25,000 for replacement of the
concrete plaza at the back of the cafeteria area. RSU 5 has a window of
opportunity for replacing the concrete between June 25-July 13 where
events will not be occurring at the competition field or the track.
6. John explained that the owner had approved a requisition in the amount of
$177,229 for release of retainage.
7. The final contract amount was $10,790,006.10.
8. Michelle Lickteig explained there was $198,000 left at the end of the project
unspent.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
9. The Committee agreed to develop a list of items where unspent money could
be used to make improvements that were missed during the project.
Priorities were as follows
• 1st Priority: Security and handicap access
• 2nd Priority: Educational needs
• 3rd Priority: Major items that would not typically be done with
normal capital improvement dollars.
10. Warranty item at the hill at the front of the school there is grass and weeds
coming up through the mulch. This needs to be put on the warranty list.
11. There was discussion about the outside door to the ski room which should
be put on an open-door notification system.
12. The hallway floor outside the library was mentioned for upgrades.
13. There was discussion about the need for theatre storage, as well as a
stagecraft room. Suggestion was made to look at the two-classroom
portable to see if that could be converted to theatre storage and stagecraft.
An additional overhead rolling door may need to be added to make it
useable for stagecraft.
14. There was talk about the track and field project and the spacing for cars is
too narrow near the tennis courts. This is the responsibility of the track and
field project.
15. Jen Gulko noted that teachers love the rolling tables and storage cabinets in
their classrooms.
16. The question was asked if the school wanted to reintroduce seating outside
of the food court. The answer was no.
17. Jen explained the cafeteria wall is being scratched by students with
backpacks waiting to get out to the bus loop at the end of the day.
18. There was a question if there are some undelivered corkboards. PDT and
Suzanne to check.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 2 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
19. Is it possible to change the color of the wall at the cafeteria and paint the
cafeteria with gloss paint? Suzanne to check and make a recommendation.
20. There was a recommendation that the foot bars at the counter in the
cafeteria take too much weight as people stand on them and break them. It
was suggested the foot bars can only work if it is a compression bracket that
is supported by the floor.
21. There was an acknowledgement that the RSU 5 School Board had moved
$161,000 from the building project to the track and field project a year
ago (2017).
22. The Committee developed a list of items where additional money could be
spent to make improvements. The list is as follows:
• Add an additional handicap access paddle at the exterior ramp to
open the first exterior door only.
• Add a positioning switch to the ski room door so the main office
knows when the door is left open.
• Install a notification panel to be connected for all door positioning
switches so when doors are left open the main office will be notified.
• Make improvements to the flooring outside the library toilets.
• Add rolling door at the portable classrooms for stagecraft and
theatre.
• Review window shades and window coverings to confirm that all
necessary window coverings have been installed.
• Final wear coat paving in the teacher’s parking lot.
• Add foot bar supports at the food court counter.
23. The Building Committee will reconvene in May to review the list and pricing
for items listed above.
24. The meeting was adjourned at 8:30 a.m.

Freeport High School

February 1, 2017 at 7:30 a.m.

please notify PDT Architects within three (3) working days.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. The meeting was called to order at 7:00 a.m.
2. The Building Committee reviewed the January 4, 2017 Building Committee
meeting notes and recommended the following changes and corrections.
 At attendees David Watts should be listed as RSU 5 not RSU 8.
 Item #18: Add the following sentence: The Committee asked the
question if there was any illumination or lighting shown on the sign
out front or if there would be any lighting on the sign by the dining
commons. PDT answered there was no illumination and no sign
lighting included in the construction documents.
 A motion was made by Becky Foley and seconded by David Smail
to approve the notes. The meeting notes were approved
unanimously with the corrections noted.
3. John Simoneau gave a report on the RSU 5 Board of Directors action taken
on official expenditures. The RSU Board approved everything, including
additional cabinets for the teachers, additional monies for furniture,
additional monies for landscaping at the front of the school, and simplified
bathrooms back at the library.
PDT Action 4. There was discussion of what would or would not be done at the loading
dock. There was concern the appearance of the old loading dock would
distract from the front of the school. PDT designers will review and report at
the next Building Committee meeting.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 4

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
5. It was confirmed that no work was being done to the lockers or locker tops in
the existing building. There was general discussion about how many of the
students use lockers. The principal stated the very few students were using
lockers because their laptops are small enough to be carried in backpacks
and purses.
6. Seniors are getting first crack at the new lockers in the new addition, then
juniors, sophomores and, lastly, freshmen.
7. There was discussion that possibly in the future, lockers could be removed,
especially if they were damaged because they don’t need one locker per
each student. Dennis will review damaged lockers and make
recommendations as to whether or not any banks of lockers can or should
be removed.
8. John Simoneau went over a small list of change orders that will be reviewed
at the next pay requisition meeting. These include adding switching and
dimming for lights, adding additional louvers and heat at the sprinkler riser
entrance room.
9. John explained Pay Requisition #17 was just recently received. It is for
payment of $123,366, which represents 92% completion of the project.
10. Dennis Ouelette made a brief report on security. He noted cameras are
going in now. There is a total of 33 cameras. There will be some new
pathways that need to be created because some cameras were moved from
the original locations.
11. Dennis explained the card key readers would be done by the end of the
week.
12. Ski Room will have a card reader, as will the Dining Commons bus entrance.
There was discussion of using wireless card readers at difficult locations
such as the Ski Room.
13. Dennis also explained that high security keys have been installed now at
critical exterior doors.
14. Principal Jen Gulko explained they would like to be able to lock bathrooms to
reduce vandalism and reduce the cleanup load on custodians. She said the
dressing room also has a small bathroom that should be locked during the
day.

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
August 30, 2016 at 7:30 a.m.
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, John Simoneau/Committee Chairman, Kelly Wentworth, Business Manager/RSU 5,
Ben Jamo, David Watts, Julie Coleman, Becky Foley, Superintendent/RSU 5, Jennifer Gulko, Principal/
Freeport High School, David Smail/FHS Physics, and Kim LaMarre/Committee Member.
Purpose: Monthly Building Committee Meeting
These notes were prepared by Lyndon Keck to the best of his ability. If there are any oversights
please notify PDT Architects within three (3) working days.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. The Building Committee’s first order of business was to convene at the front
entrance of the school and do a tour of the existing school, as well as the
new addition. Hardhats were provided for all. The tour was led by John
Simoneau and Lyndon Keck.
2. The tour went down Corridors 100 and 200. The tour included the new
nurse’s clinic, teacher’s room alterations, new bathrooms serving teachers
and health clinic, hallway light upgrades in the corridors, and classrooms
receiving renovated finishes.
3. Corridor 100 tour included new bathrooms with new finishes and new paint
on existing tile.
4. At the boy’s bathroom it was noted a privacy screen would need to be added
to block the view of the boy’s urinals.
5. At Future’s Lab 101 there was a question about how extensive the orange
paint would be along the three walls.
6. It was noted that the existing lockers being kept were in good condition,
except for the sloped locker tops which have been badly dented and
disfigured over the years. Lyndon mentioned that perhaps something could
be done to provide new tops for the lockers.
7. The group looked at typical classrooms with new carpet, new lights and
paint.
8. It was explained that the existing Whiteboards were being remounted
because the new Whiteboards would not arrive until the first or second week
of October.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
9. It was noted that existing doors had not yet been replaced with new wood
doors and new hardware. This would be done over the next two months
during after school hours.
10. The tour visited the Learning Center which had new ceilings, lights, carpet
and paint, as well as the construction of two new seminar conference rooms.
11. The tour continued on to the guidance suite were it was noted the guidance
counselors had concerns about the fabric on the chairs being “too busy”.
There was general discussion about waiting until the rooms were 100%
complete before determining if chairs should be moved to other locations.
12. The tour went on into the new building where progress included a substantial
amount of new gypsum wallboard which was receiving tape and joint
compound. Painting was beginning for priming.
13. The tour went downstairs and went through the food court, kitchen servery,
fitness rooms, health room and music room. The Building Committee also
went outside and looked at the proposed grass and tree plaza at the back of
the cafeteria.
14. The base asphalt had been placed for the bus drop off and the student
parking lot.
15. The group looked at the potential location for Freeport High School signage
on the exterior cafeteria wall.
16. The group walked around to the front of the school and looked at the
signage on the existing Freeport Performing Arts Center, as well as the
existing painted wood sign above the administrative area.
17. The Building Committee went inside and had a brief update from John
Simoneau about the status of the work. He explained there would be a pay
requisition meeting later in the day. The requisition approved by the
architect was for $915,223 and represented 71.5% completion of the project.
In addition, Change Order #10, which contained thirteen (13) change
proposal items totaling $28,761 was approved by the Construction Team.
Individual change proposals approved by the Construction Team included
two credit deducts for eliminating gypsum wallboard soffit at a corridor and
changing the variable air volume systems for the kitchen hood. Additional
work approved included adding water shut-off valves for bathrooms,
additional failed glazing units in the 1960 classroom building, changing

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
ductwork and sprinklers at the Learning Commons ceiling (which had been
previously approved by the Building Committee) adding ventilation to the
bathrooms (Women’s 100 and Men’s 100), relocating existing ductwork in
the guidance suite, and revising toilet room finishes in Women’s and Men’s
100 bathrooms.
Also a Change Proposal 088, in the amount of $6,577, was approved to
replace pipe insulation which had been removed as part of the asbestos
removal program in June. The new pipe insulation was asbestos-free and
completes the thermal encapsulation of heating and how water pipes.
18. There was a recommendation that new shades need to be added on the
south side of the classrooms of the 200 wing where shades had been
removed or lost during the reglazing of windows.

new track. They talked about cost increases the Committee had been
wrestling with related to soils and re-grading.
They also mentioned it would not be possible to have a referendum question
on the track and fields in November; it would likely happen in the winter

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
August 3, 2016
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, John Simoneau/Committee Chairman, Kelly Wentworth, Business Manager/RSU 5,
Ben Jamo, David Watts, Julie Coleman, Becky Foley, Superintendent/RSU 5, Jennifer Gulko, FHS
Principal, David Smail/FHS Physics, Craig Sickels/FHS Athletic Director and Tim Gidding
Purpose: Monthly Building Committee Meeting
These notes were prepared by Lyndon Keck to the best of his ability. If there are any oversights
please notify PDT Architects within three (3) working days.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. The meeting was called to order by John Simoneau at 7:35 a.m. in the
cafeteria at the Freeport Middle School.
2. Lyndon passed out a copy of the agenda, a copy of Brook Plummer’s
monthly summary of construction progress, and copy of the spreadsheet for
requested building upgrades to Committee members.
3. John provided a summary of the status of the project. He explained
sheetrock was mostly finished on the entrance level and prime coat of
painting was occurring. He talked about the ceiling grid being put up in the
library and 100 hallway. He commented that circular lights had been
installed at the intersection outside the toilet room.
4. John explained asbestos abatement was done during July. He talked about
the men’s and women’s #100 restrooms where plumbing rough-in was
underway.
5. New Guidance Area had a number of hidden conditions. There are a
number of change orders related to utilities hidden in the walls which needed
to be moved to make the floor plan work. John mentioned the exterior brick
was mostly completed.
6. Two windows had failed in late June. These were repaired by Marvin,
retested, and passed inspection.
7. Testing windows now is more common than it used to be during
construction. Marvin is a quality window manufacturer.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 5

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
8. Reviewed progress of electrical where LED lights had mostly been installed
in the classroom and new windows installed in the library.
9. John stated the schedule appeared be in good shape based on the report
from the construction superintendent at the pay requisition which had
happened on Tuesday 8/02/16.
10. Change Order #8 had been approved in June and Change Order #9 had
been approved in July. He gave a brief overview of the items included on
each change order.
11. John also explained some of the change orders related to shut off valves in
the #100 men’s and women’s restrooms, along with Dennis’ request to
upgrade ventilation in the bathrooms, and adding a drip edge for the
maintenance building.
12. Questions were asked about the window testing. Lyndon and John
explained the window testing is set up with a vacuum system that replicates
wind drive rain at 45-50 MPH over a sustained period. A question was
asked if it was the window unit that leaked or the installation was. John
explained it was the window unit itself which had been manufactured in the
factory and not the installation by Sheridan Corporation.
13. Lyndon gave the architect’s report of the progress of the work. He explained
the pay requisition current payment was $1,101,000 and this represented
62.6% completion of the entire project
14. Change Order #8 was $83,470. Change Order #9 was for $52,014. The
largest items on both change orders were for major items which had been
approved as “building upgrades” by the Building Committee. These included
access card readers at doors, kitchen changes requested by the Food
Service Director, and upgrades at the library learning commons
15. Lyndon talked about two items where final numbers had come in on the
requested building upgrade.
Item #3, final price for the library ceiling and light, was $74,019. The original
estimate which had been approved by the Building Committee was for
$75,000 +/-.
The other item was the electrical pathways for cameras, security system,
and access doors. The final price was $14,482. Lyndon explained the
Building Committee had previously approved $7,500 +/-.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 2 of 5

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
16. Lyndon summarized prices which had been received from Sheridan
Corporation and Crooker Construction for items which the Building
Committee had requested. Lyndon explained adding additional landscape
plantings and mulch at the front of the building was estimated at $15,277;
new picnic tables and granite benches, as well as backless seats $13,700,
adding a concrete plaza behind the food court by the bus drop $4,590
Lyndon explained there was also a quote from Welch Signage to install an
exterior post and bracket system for banners on the front lawn. The quote
was for $4,465. He explained this item could be reviewed to see if a more
affordable system could be installed
17. The Building Committee confirmed they did not want to spend money on
additional landscaping at this time. Lyndon explained Crooker needed to
know if revisions were going to be made to the back plaza. Lyndon
explained there was an area approximately 32’ x 18’ which was intended to
be loam and grass seed. This was proposed by Pat Carroll to be a concrete
plaza so students sitting at the picnic tables and benches would not trample
the grass into a mud field.
18. Committee members preferred not to do the concrete and suggested doing a
stone dust product in lieu of the top topsoil and loam. PDT to investigate
with Pat Carroll and make a recommendation for a product.
19. There was a question about the FPAC door and piano garage and when the
door will be installed.
20. David Watts explained that, in his opinion, the work being done to the FPAC
was a “disaster” throughout the summer. Air conditioning was not
operational. There was a substantial amount of dust on the chairs as a
result of holes being put through the FPAC wall.
21. Lyndon explained that construction documents owned letters that said “Café
Freeport” on the back wall of the kitchen servery area. These are 12” high
brushed aluminum letters pin mounted on the herringbone pattern tile.
22. Committee members felt the lettering was not needed. There is so much
artwork in the space that it would be too confusing. People in the food
court/cafeteria should already know it is a café area. These are 12” high
brushed aluminum letters pin mounted on the herringbone pattern tile.
23. Lyndon showed a sketch and explained the contract documents own two (2)
sets of letters, 12” high brushed aluminum letters, pin mounted on the rain
canopies to either side of the new main entrance. Lyndon stated the design
team thought it was better to just have one set of letters and put it on the
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 3 of 5

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
brick wall up high where it would not be susceptible to vandalism and where
it could be seen from a greater distance.
24. Lyndon and the design team suggested the other set of letters could be put
around the back by the bus drop off area. Committee members chose to
wait before deciding whether to ask for a credit or relocate the second set of
letters.
25. There was a question about the font type. Lyndon explained there is already
a set of letters on the FPAC which are “Sans Serif” which, in Lyndon’s
opinion, was not especially attractive.
The Committee voted to wait and discuss this item in September when they
could see photographs of the existing FPAC lettering to determine where
they want to locate various exterior signage.
26. There was general discussion about security and how secure the building
would be when it opens in September. It was suggested PDT do a walk
through with the School Resource Officer and Dennis when he comes back
from vacation.
27. There was a question about electricity service serving field hockey. It used
to be mounted on the portable classrooms and is now temporarily mounted
on a wooden post.
28. The athletic director explained the field hockey field sod had not been
properly installed. Edges were curled and it was placed on top of seeded
areas. He stated this would not allow for playing on the field this fall. (PDT
checked on this item after the meeting and discovered the sod was being
installed independently and not part of Sheridan’s contract. Maybe Dennis
had contracted with someone else to do the sod. The existing drawings
show for topsoil, loam, and seeding want watering but not sod.
29. There was discussion about how dusty the building was and would
professional cleaners be coming through.
30. John mentioned the gym, in particular, did not have proper dust protection
and it should be professionally cleaned.
31. PDT to talk to Sheridan Construction about cleaning and dust protection.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 4 of 5

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
32. John suggested that before renovations occur in the spring and summer of
2017 there be a preconstruction meeting to go over proper dust protection
so extensive dust throughout the building does not occur next spring and
summer
33. The meeting was adjourned at approximately 9:00 a.m.
Next Building Committee meeting will be held on August 30, 2016 at
7:30 a.m.
END OF MEETING NOTES

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
June 1, 2016
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, John Simoneau/Committee Chairman, Dennis Ouelette, Owner’s Representative
/RSU 5, Kelly Wentworth, Business Manager/RSU 5, Brian Campbell/FHS Principal, Ben Jamo,
Kevin Nadeau, David Watts, Julie Coleman, Catherine Breer, Tim Giddings, Ed McDonough,
Superintendent/RSU 5 and Freeport High School Resource Officer Mike McManus
Purpose: Monthly Building Committee Meeting
These notes were prepared by Lyndon Keck to the best of his ability. If there are any oversights
please notify PDT Architects within three (3) working days.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. The meeting was called to order by John Simoneau at 7:37 a.m.
2. John did a summary of the status of the project. He explained the pay
requisition and change orders would not happen until Thursday at 2:00 p.m.
He expected the pay requisition to be about $1,194,000 with the project
about 38% complete
3. Lyndon mentioned there was going to be at least one item, as a change
order, which was about $7,500 to remove stumps, unsuitable material and
ledge from the waterline that was installed over April vacation.
4. The Building Committee expected to walk the job site at the end of the
meeting, therefore, there was no construction update.
5. John led the Committee through the matrix for requested building upgrades.
He reviewed items which had been previously approved over the last six
months.
6. The first item of discussion was replacing the ceiling, lighting, and flooring in
the library. The architects had not yet received a firm quote from Sheridan
Corporation for ceiling and lighting. Lyndon estimated the cost was going to
be around $60,000-$65,000. The Committee agreed to approve new ceiling,
lighting and carpet for the library with the understanding that almost
$125,000 of value engineering had occurred in January 2015 when these
items were taken out of the original project scope in an effort to deal with
increased inflation.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
7. There were comments that the library was always intended to be renovated.
The library would be one of the few spaces which would be visited by all of
the students and regularly by the public. It should have a facelift along with
the existing classrooms.
8. There was discussion about item #5 to renovate the library bathrooms at a
cost of $105,000. After much discussion the Committee agreed to have PDT
issue another value engineered design which would result in three unisex
bathrooms in their present location with new finishes and new toilet fixtures.
Lyndon had estimated the cost would be closer to the $56,000 that was in
the original December 2015 budget.
9. The Committee confirmed they would hold off on any increased monies for
furniture until the end of the project.
10. The Committee agreed to hold off on landscaping and outdoor seating and
tables until the end of the project. Lyndon noted they had developed
sketches and sent them on to Sheridan for pricing during the month of May
but PDT had not received any hard prices yet.
11. The Committee discussed item #16-security cameras, devices and wiring
which was a quotation from Exactitude, a vendor who would work directly for
RSU 5.
12. Exactitude had provided two prices, one for high resolution cameras with
wiring at $79,300 and a second price for low resolution cameras with wiring
at $61,000.
13. The Committee agreed to approve and recommend security cameras,
devices and wiring to the RSU 5 School Board with an upset budget of
$79,300. They also agreed that Dennis Ouelette and School Resource
Officer McManus would review cameras to determine which ones really
needed to be high resolution and which could be low resolution.
14. Lyndon recommended this item be approved so wiring could be installed.
Installation needs to happen now while the actual installation of the cameras
could wait for another year.
15. The Building Committee discussed item #18-to provide new Medco high
security locksets at all exterior doors and provide new, keyed cylinders for all
existing building interior doors. Cost was $24,065. This item was approved
unanimously by the Committee.
16. The Committee discussed item #19-keyless entries for access control and
security alarms for the entire school at sixteen (16) exterior doors at a cost of
$62,100. The Committee agreed that security was a high priority for all of
the schools and this was an important security item recommended by the
School Resource Officer McManus, as well as administration. The
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 2 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
Committee unanimously approved item #19 and item #20-electric pathways
for items #17 and #19.
17. There was discussion about putting carpet in the library. There was a
question as to whether carpet was the right material or should it remain as
vinyl tile. Lyndon explained almost every library in a public school has
carpet. It was further explained the carpet would be carpet tile; there would
be extra stock given to Dennis so if there were particular wear areas the
facility management group could replace the carpet tile.
18. PDT needs to issue the final security drawings to the School Resource
Officer who said he had not seen the latest drawings.
19. PDT to prepare drawings for the value engineered bathroom upgrades for
the three bathrooms outside the library. The role of the three bathrooms
near the library would be diminished as a result of increased bathrooms
throughout the whole facility.
20. The Building Committee did a tour of the construction site. They started at
the new bus loop and walked in through the doors leading to the food court.
They walked through the food court, fitness rooms, music room, and
keyboard room and went up the western staircase to the second floor where
they viewed some of the classrooms, teacher workroom, new bathroom and
new hallway connected to the original 1961 building.
21. The building tour lasted about 30 minutes. At the end of the tour Lyndon
suggested the Committee might want to tour the building every sixty (60)
days. A lot of progress will be happening during the sixty day time period.
22. The meeting was adjourned at approximately 9:00 a.m.
Next Building Committee meeting will be held on July 6, 2016 at 7:30 a.m.
END OF MEETING NOTES
