Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
April 6, 2016
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, Kim LaMarre/Committee Member, John Simoneau/Committee Chairman,
Catherine Breer/Committee Member, Dennis Ouelette, Owner’s Representative/RSU 5,
Kelly Wentworth,Business Manager/RSU 5, David Smail/Committee Member, Brian
Campbell/FHS Principal, Ben Jamo, David Steckler, Craig Sickels, Athletic Director/RSU 5,
Ed McDonough,Superintendent/RSU 5 and Suzanne Morin, PDT Architects
Purpose: Building Committee Meeting
These notes were prepared by Lyndon Keck to the best of his ability. If there are any oversights
please notify PDT Architects within three (3) working days.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. The meeting was called to order by John Simoneau at 7:35 a.m.
2. John gave a recap for the Committee of work done to date including
Tuesday’s pay requisition meeting and approval of Pay Requisition #5 in the
amount of $375,974.
3. There was also a signed Change Order #4 in the amount of $4,742. There
was also a change order with money for a steel beam missed by the
architects and engineers, the substitution of concrete sealer in lieu of painted
concrete in storage rooms, bathroom demolition near the locker rooms, and
the addition of pendant mounted light fixtures in the existing building
classrooms to avoid conflict with surface-mounted conduit on the underside
of the plaster ceilings.
4. John also explained there were several credits: one related to eliminating
unnecessary conduit and one related to deleting drywall from the interior
walls of the maintenance building.
5. John explained the structural steel had been completed this month. The
contractor is installing steel bar joists to support the metal deck formwork for
the concrete floors. Exterior steel studs were erected around the perimeter
of the building. The contractor is hoping to enclose the building and make it
watertight by May 15, 2016.
6. Lyndon provided an update related to on-going work. This included
continuing to work on the security and hardware upgrades that had been
requested by the Building Committee, as well as the restroom renovations in
the library. The restroom renovations have been forwarded to Sheridan for
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
pricing. It is PDT’s goal to have pricing completed for video, security and
hardware for the Building Committee’s May meeting.
7. Lyndon brought samples of the aluminum finish for the Marvin Windows.
This aluminum finish matches the aluminum finish of the FPAC windows
which were manufactured by a different manufacturer. Lyndon explained the
aluminum finish was a premium finish which costs an additional $8,000.
This change had been approved by the Construction Committee earlier in
the month.
Lyndon also explained the Food Service Director had made a number of
requests for changes and upgrades to the kitchen. PDT now has a
quotation from Sheridan in the amount of $17,800 for the various
improvements. The Building Committee requested the breakdown be
presented in May so they could make decisions as to which kitchen related
items would be recommended for approval.
8. Suzanne presented a bid tab analysis for the Freeport High School
furnishing bids. There were five vendors that responded to the various bid
packages.
Suzanne organized the bid amounts and the award options according to low
bid by group, overall bid by primary vendor, and overall bid by primary
vendor with State contract bid alternate. Suzanne recommended that
Wenger Corporation be awarded the music and instrument shelving bid in
the amount of $39,016. She recommended W. B. Mason be awarded the
fireproof files bid, via the Greater Portland Council of Government contract,
in the amount of $10,547.82. Suzanne recommended Creative Office
Pavilion be awarded the furnishings contract with Bid Alternate #1 for Hokki
stools, Bid Alternate #2 for library shelving, and Bid Alternate #3 for library
furnishings for a total bid amount of $396,931.96. The grand total amount,
including all three bid items, is $446,495.78.
Suzanne explained this means the furnishings which were placed out to bid
came in under budget by $46,680.22. This bid also includes extra library
furniture to complete the library package.
9. Suzanne led a discussion of color for furniture. She showed the colors
which were being recommended for classroom chairs. After much
discussion, four colors were chosen with the fourth color being black. It was
agreed the chairs would mixed colors in all of the classrooms so every
classroom would get one of the four colors.
10. Suzanne also showed fabric for pedestal files and for the teacher’s chairs.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 2 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
11. The Committee agreed by consensus to accept the bids for the furniture, as
per Suzanne’s recommendations and for the overall bid to go to Creative
Office Pavilion.
12. Suzanne showed color changes which had been recommended by
Catherine Breer and Julie Coleman. Suzanne noted some color was
eliminated and some walls changed to be neutral, especially in the area of
the food court where artwork was to be mounted on the walls.
13. The Committee voted unanimously to approve the colors which had been
selected with the changes proposed by Suzanne, Catherine and Julie.
14. Kim LaMarre explained the Class of 2014 was interested in contributing
$8,000-$10,000 for some outdoor furniture seating or tables. Kim requested
PDT make some proposals for appropriate outdoor furnishings, either
outside the food court or at the front entrance.
15. Kim also explained the PTSO was interested in having two posts erected at
the front of the school where informational banners could be hung. The
posts would be 10’-12’ apart. PDT will ask Pat Carroll his advice for a good
location for the banner posts. PDT to get a price from Sheridan to erect the
posts.
16. A Committee member asked if PDT had gotten the answer on locker reuse.
Lyndon said no, but will attempt to get an answer for the May meeting.
17. John reiterated the importance of having a completed price from various
contractors for their proposed additional work items at the May meeting. He
also suggested this will take up a substantial amount of Committee
discussion and the Building Committee should consider moving their outdoor
building tour to the June meeting date. John requested pricing be delivered
to the Committee prior to the May meeting.
18. It was recommended that if the Building Committee could determine their
choices for the expenditure of additional monies. The recommendations
could be made at the RSU 5 School Board meeting on May 11, 2016,
otherwise the recommendations could not go until June 8, 2016 or June 5,
2016.
19. The meeting was adjourned at approximately 9:05 a.m.
END OF MEETING NOTES

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School, 7:30 a.m.-8:35 a.m.
Project:
February 3, 2016
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, Kim LaMarre/Committee Member, Julie Coleman/Committee Member, John
Simoneau/Committee Chairman, Catherine Breer/Committee Member, Kevin Nadeau/Committee
Member, Ed McDonough, Superintendent of School/RSU 5, Dennis Ouelette, Owner’s
Representative/RSU 5, Kelly Wentworth, Business Manager/RSU 5, David Smail/Committee
Member, Brian Campbell/FHS Principal, David Watts/FPAC and Committee Member
Purpose: Building Committee Meeting
These notes were taken by Lyndon Keck. Should you find discrepancies, please notify PDT
Architects as soon as possible.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. No action was taken on the Building Committee meeting notes for January 6,
2016.
2. Changes to January 6, 2016 Meeting Notes: John Simoneau wanted the
previous month’s notes corrected to show there was discussion about
hardware changes, security cameras, and door lock notification systems that
were to be reviewed. At the January Building Committee meeting Lyndon
notified the Committee there had been a meeting over the December
holidays to discuss upgrading hardware and security systems.
David Watts was also present at the January 6, 2016 Building Committee
meeting.
3. John Simoneau opened the meeting by informing the Committee of progress
to date, including removal of unsuitable soils, footing and foundation
concrete work, backfilling and excavation work. He also informed the
Committee of two changes orders that had been processed and one pay
requisition meeting in January.
4. Lyndon confirmed John’s report. He said the work was progressing
satisfactorily. The last requisition was Application #3 where current payment
due was $518,543. Lyndon also explained that there had been an additional
1,000 cubic yards of additional, unsuitable material removed inside the
building footprint, primarily where the Industrial Arts building previously
stood. This represented an additional change order cost of approximately
$29,000-$30,000.
5. Lyndon shared progress being made by the Hardware & Security
Subcommittee. He explained there was a progress drawing that showed
new locations for computerized key fobs, new compartmentalization panic
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 5

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
doors, new cameras and existing door lock replacements. Lyndon also
explained that a system was going to be priced that would be a notification
system for all exterior doors allowing administrative personnel knowledge
when an exterior door was open or left open by mistake.
6. A Committee member requested these drawings be made available to the
Committee members electronically.
7. Lyndon explained that Suzanne had delivered furniture at the high school for
the teachers and staff to test drive, use and comment on. Many of the
pieces of furniture were in the library. The principal, Brian Campbell, briefly
described the furniture teachers preferred and where various tables and
chairs were intended to go. Committee members made their own comments
regarding preferences. Committee members were reminded that an email
with last month’s PowerPoint went out to all Committee members so they
can review Suzanne’s presentation in more detail.
8. After the Committee meeting, Julie Coleman recommended that colors for
chairs be dark colors for the armrests and seats. Samples that have been
delivered were mostly white and were already showing scuff marks and dirt.
9. Lyndon explained that Suzanne would be finishing the development of the
furniture specifications for bidding in February. A final presentation will be
made to the Committee members at their next meeting on Wednesday,
March 2, 2016. Lyndon also explained Suzanne was gathering color
samples for paint, carpet, vinyl tile, lockers, toilet partitions, plastic laminate,
and casework and will be making a presentation at the March meeting.
Lyndon stressed it would be important for all Committee members to try to
attend this meeting; PDT will need to make approvals of colors and return
them to the general contractor in March and April.
10. A question was asked about how much money had been suggested as an
upgrade for security hardware, cameras and hardware system. The
Committee members noted $75,000 had been reserved for keyless entries
and security alarms and another $7,500 for rekeying existing interior doors.
11. Lyndon was asked if they’d done any more engineering work on gutting and
renovating student bathrooms across from the library where a placeholder of
$56,825 had been reserved. Lyndon said no. The Committee members
said this had been approved for PDT to begin working on.
12. A Committee member asked if there had been an update made on any of
the cost estimates to the preliminary estimates passed out in December.
Lyndon said no, but as work was being accomplished we could update the
estimates. Lyndon to deliver upgraded estimates at the next meeting.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 2 of 5

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
13. Lyndon was asked if there were any updates on technology. Lyndon said he
didn’t know of any. Ed McDonough, however, said he had spoken with Seth
and Seth was either within budget or below budget for technology. Seth’s
preference is to use short throw projectors for the existing classrooms.
14. The question was asked if the existing classrooms were being painted in the
100 and 200 Wing. Lyndon said yes.
15. Catherine Breer made an update presentation on the art program inside the
school. Her group recommends removal of the tile mural that presently
exists outside of the administration entrance. The group also recommends
moving the trophy case.
16. Catherine explained there are four planned projects. Additional artists have
been brought on board. The art is as follows:
 Circular art shapes to be located within the cafeteria/food court.
 Square forms to be painted.
 One large rectangular mural to be located above the cubbies
between the stair and food court on the lower level.
 Photography to be printed onto aluminum or metal panels via a
juried selection.
17. Catherine explained that the Art Committee’s hope is that the art teachers
and students will be involved and the projects will be student driven art with
help from professional artists and art teachers.
18. Catherine is hoping to work with PDT to choose colors in the areas where
the artwork could occur so the colors would not clash between the artwork
and building background.
19. Some pieces of art may need blocking in the walls and additional lighting.
PDT to investigate. Lyndon was asked what the cost of art lighting might
cost and he said between $4,000-$15,000.
20. The question was asked if the FPAC lobby was available for either trophy
cases or art. General sense is that it is not.
21. There was discussion about the trophy case and the concept of trying to
continue with trophy cases. A question was posed as to “where do we put
everything”? Catherine suggested trophies and elements related to awards
for academic excellence might be rotated by staff.
22. A suggestion was made to photograph all existing murals or artwork in the
high school so there would be a visual record if this work is to be displaced.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 3 of 5

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
23. David Watts talked about Camden High School which has “walls of fame” for
academic and scholastic achievement. These are simple with small bronze
nameplates that don’t take up too much room within the building.
24. There was discussion about the importance of preserving the school history
and noted that alumni have an investment in their school memories. The
Committee should be careful to find ways to preserve those connections,
whether they be athletic or scholastic.
25. There was discussion about how other schools handle memorabilia related
to sports and academics. PDT Architects to make a brief summary of how
other schools are solving the space problem for trophies, artwork and
scholastic honors.
26. The mural by the administrative wing was constructed in 1986. It was
attached directly to the concrete block wall with epoxy construction adhesive.
It will be almost impossible to removal the mural and keep it intact. It will
very likely be destroyed because to the strength of the adhesive glues.
27. Catherine explained the theme of the artwork was “community and
environment”. Whether it is artwork of photos they want to encourage local
people to be involved. They want local photographers to submit
photographs taken within the three towns. There was discussion about
approaching the Tri-Town newspaper to see if they would be willing to do a
press release to the citizens in Pownal, Durham and Freeport.
28. There was a motion made to endorse the concept of removing the tile mural.
The motion was unanimously approved.
29. The next The next Building Committee meeting will be March 2, 2016 from
7:30 a.m.-9:00 a.m. and will have an emphasis on interior colors and
furnishings
30. The meeting was adjourned at 8:35 a.m.
END OF MEETING NOTES

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
January 6, 2016 7:30 a.m.-8:45 a.m.
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, Suzanne Morin/PDT Architects, Kim LaMarre/Committee Member, Julie
Coleman/Committee Member, Ben Jamo/Committee Member, John Simoneau/Committee
Chairman, Kevin Nadeau/Committee Member, Ed McDonough, Superintendent of Schools/RSU
5, Dennis Ouellettte, Owner’s Representative/RSU 5, Kelly Wentworth, Business Manager/RSU
5, David Smail/Committee Member and FHS Faculty Member and Brian Campbell/FHS Principal
Purpose: Building Committee Meeting
These notes were taken by Lyndon Keck. Should you find discrepancies, please notify PDT
Architects as soon as possible.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. John Simoneau opened the meeting and asked if anyone had any
comments on the December 2, 2015 Building Committee meeting notes. No
exceptions were taken.
2. John Simoneau updated the Committee on the status of the construction.
3. Lyndon updated the Committee on the work from the architect’s perspective.
Lyndon passed out a colored 8-1/2” x 11” sheet showing the status of the
concrete pouring for footings and foundations as of January 5, 2016.
Lyndon described several meetings which had occurred with S. W. Cole
Engineering, the geotechnical engineers, with Sheridan and PDT Architects
to review unsuitable soils, ledge calculations, and the protection of concrete
and sub-grade soils from frost and freezing.
4. Lyndon reported on unsuitable soil removed to date. He explained the RSU
owned the removal of 2,000 cubic yards of unsuitable material. The
geotechnical engineering report had shown there would be substantial
unsuitable material in the area of the bus loop, kitchen entrance, as well as
unsuitable soils in the area of footings and foundations for the new addition.
5. Lyndon explained that the contract documents contain 100 cubic yards of
open ledge removal and disposal. No open ledge had been removed to
date. The contract also includes an allowance for 80 cubic yards of trench
ledge removal and disposal. To date, 52.55 cubic yards of ledge has been
removed. We are not expecting any further ledge removal in the back part of
the site although we are anticipating ledge removal at the front entry of the
parent drop-off for work to be done in the future.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
6. Lyndon briefly explained that PDT Architects was working with Mark
Coleman, an asbestos abatement consultant, who works for RSU 5. Mark
has maintained records of asbestos identification and removal for the District
for many years. Mark Coleman had done three different test locations in the
existing 1960 building with Paul White Tile, who will be the flooring
subcontractor, to determine what the condition of the mastic was in several
different locations.
Lyndon explained that asbestos would be removed by the owner’s asbestos
removal contractor over the February, April and summer breaks. Asbestos
removal in the existing building is complicated because there are numerous
flooring conditions where asbestos tile has been removed but the mastic has
not been removed. PDT will be working with Mark Coleman over the next
month to determine how best to proceed with removal in individual areas of
the project.
7. Suzanne Morin made a Powerpoint presentation on furnishings. In the base
project there is $504,000 for new furnishings. Suzanne had broken the
proposed furnishings down by phases. She showed the individual amount
allowed for each of the areas, whether it be music, food court, nurse, art
classrooms or administrative areas.
8. Suzanne also showed individual tables and chairs, as well as furnishing floor
plans for all of the spaces being proposed.
9. Suzanne will have samples delivered to the school so that teachers and
Dennis Ouellette will have an opportunity to check on the design and
ruggedness of the furniture. For many of the classrooms there are going to
be movable tables so teachers can rearrange the tables quickly.
10. Suzanne had proposed four Hokki stools in each classroom. The Committee
suggested that perhaps two Hokki stools would be enough in every
classroom. All teachers wouldn’t necessarily want to use them. The extras
could be moved into classrooms where the teachers needed or encouraged
use of the stools.
11. Suzanne showed the Learning Commons. She showed the furniture
included in her preliminary budget which includes shelving, casual furniture,
as well as study tables and seating in the two learning labs. Suzanne
showed possible instrument and music storage. There was discussion as to
what was actually going to be required for instrument storage since there
was going to be a locked room made available for student instruments.
12. The Committee asked to review the furnishings again at a future meeting.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 2 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
13. Committee members asked if Suzanne would visit the Durham Community
School and talk with Principal Will Patten to review which pieces of furniture
worked and didn’t work at the Durham Community School.
14. The Committee also asked that Suzanne make a review of existing furniture
with Dennis Ouellette to determine how much existing furniture is acceptable
for continued reuse. The Committee didn’t want to throw out or replace
furniture which still had useful life.
.
15. Kelly Wentworth explained that the District never really threw out furniture
but would distribute furniture to other schools which needed more because
of loss or breakage. Any high school furniture would most probably be
recycled at other schools.
16. The Committee also requested Suzanne meet with some of the teacher
groups to have them review the furniture samples and demonstrations so the
teachers would have an opportunity to weigh in on individual pieces of
furniture.
17. There was a question as to whether or not there would be traditional
teacher’s desks. Suzanne explained a lot of teachers wanted to work from
tables but not the traditional pedestal desk. She also explained there were
storage units and shelving units for teachers to be able to replace drawers
and file cabinets where necessary.
18. The meeting was adjourned at approximately 8:45 a.m.
END OF MEETING NOTES

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
June 3, 2015
Date:
Attendees: Kathy Cogan, PDT
Building Committee
Purpose: Building Committee Meeting 07
Topic Agenda/Notes
Update on 1. PDT updated progress on DEP, Army Corps of Engineers, and Town
Regulatory approvals
Approvals
- DEP permit received
- ACOE approval received
- Project Review Board hearing at 6pm 7/8, but expected to be pushed to
next meeting at the end of the month pending resolution of the entry
loop and memorial tree
Update on ROW / 2. Easement and responsibility language has been ironed out between RSU
easement attorneys and Town attorneys. First hearing at Town Council was Tuesday,
7/7, and it went very well. Vote expected by Town Council on 7/21.
Update on 3. Advertisement for solicitation of prequalification applications was posted on
General 7/6. Prequalification applications & questionnaires are due 7/17 at 4pm.
Contractor
PDT will review and evaluate submissions and make recommendations to
Prequalification
the Superintendent and Building Committee for selection of prequalified
Process
contractors. Target date to notify prequalified contractors is August 7.
Review 4. PDT reviewed proposed construction phasing and timing, including use of
Construction portable classrooms, major moves of programs, and summer work.
Schedule & - Lyndon pointed out that once a contractor is selected, this is reviewed
Phasing
with them and modified as needed for best construction sequencing
and least disruption to school functions
- Some discussion of egress access and temporary partitions discussed
at addition area. Egress will need to be maintained in this corridor;
chorus room will remain functional throughout construction of the
addition. So contractor will need to operate around school schedule
with regards to impact in this area. (eg. flying steel)
- Also discussed limit of construction and potential impact to student
parking lot.
- School will want to send notification to parents in August regarding
limited site access, traffic pattern changes, etc. Site plan with limit of
construction, laydown area, GC parking should be included in this letter.
- PDT recommends weekly meetings between the principal and GC to
coordinate activities on site throughout construction.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes
Discuss Painted 5. PDT reminded the committee that there are a handful of student murals on
Murals in classroom walls. All classroom walls are scheduled to be repainted as part
Classrooms
of the renovation scope of work.
- PDT asked that review of the existing murals be put on the agenda for
the art subcommittee that will be meeting this fall to discuss new art for
the school.
- David Smail indicated that many of the murals were done by students
solely with the permission of the teacher in that room at the time and
were not commissioned.
- PDT clarified that the tile mural at the front entry is expected to remain
and modifications in this area have worked around keeping that mural.
- Laurie Donavan requested to be appointed to the art subcommittee
Update on Track 6. Kim LaMarre updated building committee on the organization of the effort
& Turf and fundraising goals.
Fundraising - Public comment included concerns that a track had been voted down
Efforts
by the towns and that this effort was proceeding without voter approval
- Members of the building committee clarified that this is a SEPARATE
effort from the charge of the building committee and that should
sufficient funds be raised, it is up to the school board to determine how
they obtain public input and comment on the issue.
- Members of the building committee also clarified that although a track
and field are not in the scope of this project, it is the committee’s
responsibility to recognize that a local fundraising effort is underway
and to protect the bidding process of the project approved by
referendum by isolating scope of work that would be impacted by an
outside track and field scope of work
Dedicated Tree 7. PDT presented the history of the project and discovery of the memorial tree,
findings by two arborists, and two options for reconfiguring then entry drop
off zone to address the memorial tree.
- Option A replanted a new tree in the center loop. This is not preferred
by the family
- Option B reconfigured the driveway to leave the tree where it is, tree
ends up being in a newly configured island inside the loop
- Public comment included concerns from a graduating senior who
requested that the tree not be removed.
- Building Committee expressed concerns about the pine tree drop at the
parking spaces close to the tree line.
- Preliminary estimate for this change in scope is $12,500. If this is
accurate, we have funds in the contingency to cover additional costs
- Family spoke in favor of Option B. They are pleased that there is a plan
that will allow the tree to stay where it is.

- Building Committee recommended implementing fertilization and
maintenance plan outlined by arborist to prepare the tree for

that it satisfies the family and the community and that it still needs work to
satisfy budget and Project Review Board.
9. Donna Larson, Town Planner spoke with regards to the proposed Option B

community. He asked that the public remember that the committee wants
to work as a community and requested that aggressive negative responses

AGENDA

  
  
 
 
 
  
  
 


 
 



  
  
 
 




  
 


  
 
 


 






 
 

Building Committee (see attached attendance list)
Purpose: Building Committee Meeting 03
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
Schematic 1. PDT reviewed the schematic design package including exterior renderings
Design of the addition and new entry.
Package 2. The class of 2014 has some funds it would like to put toward a piece of the
project – benches at the Food Court plaza or in the Peace Garden or a new
water fountain with water bottle refill. Funds total approximately $5,000 –
$8,000.
3. Committee recommended relocating the door at the new egress stair to the
north wall so that it would be under the pop-out above for rain protection.
Energy 4. PDT is working with Thornton-Thomasetti, to run energy models and
Modeling analysis of glazing locations. Currently the project is trending to be a net
zero addition, meaning energy costs would be equal to or less than current
energy costs once the addition is complete and functional.
5. This is due to projected energy savings from installing LED lights in the
1961 building, highly efficient envelope of the new addition, and other
energy savings measures PDT is proposing.
Department 6. Food Court
Plans - Committee raised concerns about fixed seating not allowing for
maximum flexibility
- School Board Meetings held here
- High School dances held here
- Committee asked PDT to look at providing fixed seating at perimeter
- Will need to include a speaker system and AV capabilities for
alternative uses [PDT to confirm that this is in the Elec. Estimate]
Site / Parking 7. Committee asked if we can get more student parking if we don’t pave
- PDT’s experience is, No.
8. PDT clarified that the parking reduction proposed is from the referendum
plans; an overall increase from the current parking counts is still achieved
9. Committee asked about porous paving.
- Currently there is no plan to do porous paving
- Storm water management plan still needs to be submitted to the DEP
for review and approval

the scope of work to strengthen foundations and columns for future 2nd floor
expansion, estimated at $23,400.
FPAC 14. The below is an excerpt from an email from David Watts distributed on
Concerns 2/23/2015:
“. . . I believe this [the FPAC] is the only program area where there has been a
decrease in usable and functional area from what we currently have. Also, I am not
just a teacher rep on the committee. I am a community member, and I am the
manager of the FPAC for the town. So I represent hundreds of stakeholders and
have to be concerned about creating immediate functional obsolescence that will
not enable me to manage and rent the facility.
My concern is that people may not understand the problematic design issues I am
speaking of. And suggesting that we carry things a quarter of a mile means that we
should probably have committee members take part in a field trip so that they can
understand what is going to be asked of our students, teachers and community
members.
Over the past ten years, the community and school drama department has lost the
set construction area to a part-time classroom. We have been forced to use art
classrooms and the stage itself for construction, which creates a huge issue in
maintaining the space. We will now, with the new proposed building, lose all
construction area outside the FPAC itself because the art rooms are being moved
upstairs.
The FPAC put a storage pod outside as a stop gap measure and my budget has to
pay for that separately - the school does not pay toward it at all. Now we will lose
that as well. Without storage space within reasonable proximity, the space will
become essentially unusable as a theater.
We have added significant storage space within the new footprint of the building for
athletic storage. We have even added heated square footage that will be used only
for one extra-curricular activity - skiing. What the FPAC users are being asked to
survive with is the equivalent of putting storage pods on the far side of the field
hockey field and asking the athletic department to carry all of their equipment back
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 2 of 3

and forth from the gym. With the exception that their equipment does not weigh as
much as a fully constructed wall.
I am getting statements of concern already from people who rent the facility and
contribute funds to take care of the FPAC where the school does not pay. In order
to create more of a sense of transparency, I'm at least asking that these concerns
about space planning be made part of the minutes. And that if nothing is going to
be done within the scope of the project - such as creating a garage or at least a pad
that can be used to construct a garage through the use of community and FPAC
funds - then I'd like that decision to be made clear in the minutes.”

Topic Agenda/Notes Action
Budget 1. Due to inflation, project budget needs to adjust 5.5%-6% or $680K
2. PDT revised plan to reduce square footage by 3252 S.F.
- Simplify shape of 2nd floor
- Reduce extent of the perimeter walls by 13.5%
- Reduce parent drop-off parking by a few spaces
- Reduce the number of new parking spaces @ student lot
- Reduce the scope of the renovation work in the 1961 building
3. Discussed potential bid alternates (see hand-out attached)
4. The budget approved by Referendum is $14,638,009
- $11,477,000 construction costs
- 10% contingency + soft costs
New Plan 5. PDT reviewed the changes made from the referendum plans to
Review accommodate the inflation impact on the budget and address the vision
Brian Campbell has for academic houses & Learning Labs
Energy Goals 6. PDT reviewed and discussed preliminary energy targets for the project
- Discussed that we are not attempting certification in any one program
but will use various programs as guidelines to achieve a very energy
efficient building
- Committee agreed that money for certification is better spent on the
building
- PDT has suggested a new zero energy add as an additional goal.

documents for bidding
- Question was raised about trying to get bidding documents out earlier in
the summer. PDT replied that earlier than the targeted August date is
too aggressive

while building is occupied.

from the new Art classrooms

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/98358390-3677-4387-8093-af922a72ca9a

FHS Track and Field Design Committee
Friday, July 28, 2017
Freeport High School
Minutes
Attendance: John Simoneau , Brad Williams, Becky Foley, Michelle Lickteig, Rob Ponteau,
David Roussel, Fred Palmer, Kim LaMarre (by phone), John Paterson,
Guest: Cynthia Alexander
Updates:
1. Art work update: Cynthia Alexander met on June 13th with Nike, art teachers
from the high school, Craig Sickels, and Dennis Ouellette. The art designers
brought several ideas to this subgroup. Several options were reviewed. The
group wanted the art work to have visibility from the field. They discussed the
elements of nature in each of the three towns, and wanted to ensure the three
towns of the RSU would be represented. Wanted the look to be seamless. Looked
at the back wall of the high school which created the most visibility. Looked at
the retaining wall and the possibilities. On July 24th they sent back two ideas.
Cynthia shared the two ideas: one was for the back wall of the high school and
the other was for the net. Nike is willing to fund one of the two options. The
consensus of the committee was the mural on the back wall, more than the
netting option, but would like some more natural color options and possibly the
use of realistic topographical map lines. There were also questions about the
longevity of the art. Cynthia will connect with Nike about the questions from the
committee and will present the idea to the Board at the Board retreat.
2. Timeline – There was a construction meeting last week. RAD is four weeks
behind where they should be due to the retaining wall and the removal of 2655
cubic yards of unsuitable materials. This is based on truck volume. Should be
calculated based upon what is in the ground, not on the truck. October 13, 2017
is currently the final date for finishing.
3. Lights – Lights are going to be installed Monday and Tuesday of this week. On
Thursday, there is a meeting with CMP and the electrician. The needed account
with CMP has been opened.
4. Budget: The contingency is currently at about $130,000.
Considerations: Pads for the dugouts, modification of bleacher pad, conduit,
revision to bleacher pads, revision to sidewalk. By next meeting, we should
prioritize remaining contingency.
5. PA – Nothing new. Should we get pricing? We just want to know what the design
fee would be.

6. Donor Plaza – Site contractor has agreed to no mark up. Could RSU purchase the
stones directly? Pat passed out a new budget sheet for donor plaza. Need names
by September 1st.
a. 21,150 from Swinson.
b. Metal fabricator is interested in doing it with stainless steel. Putting a

FHS Track and Field Design Committee
Friday, February 10, 2017
Freeport High School
Minutes
Attendance: Dennis Ouellette, Tim Reich, Charlie Mellon , Kim LaMarre, John Simoneau, Pat
Carroll, Fred Palmer, Rob Pontau, Tom DeLois, David Roussel, Todd Coffin, Craig Sickels,
Michelle Lickteig, John Paterson, Becky Foley
Ø Any revisions of the minutes, send to Ginny. We will approve at the next meeting.
Ø Neighborhood Meeting – John Simoneau reported out on the neighborhood meeting.
Their concerns included the following: student behavior on the field, traffic issues
including respectful driving, lighting, number of events under the lights.
Ø Meeting dates are listed in agenda for today
Ø Project Review Board Meeting
a. Three members recused themselves because of their opposition to project
b. No binding votes due to not having a quorum
c. Largest hurdle is the lighting
i. Ordinance – focused on parking lot lights which are limited to 20 feet high
ii. Specialty applications could be utilized but because of the proximity to
neighborhood it will be difficult
iii. If we pull lights, we will work with the Planning Board to create variance
iv. If we are going to have lights will have to explain why they need to be 60-
80 foot high
v. Two designs: one LED vs. metal halide
vi. Landscaping could help with the lighting issue
vii. Without lights, we lose flexibility for times of games
viii. In spring, lights should not be an issue. May 1st sunset at 7:43. Light use
would be very limited.
d. Project Review Board was fairly supportive of the rest of the project
e. Pat Carroll passed out a sketch. Revised design would need to be in by next
Wednesday.
f. What to include?
i. Bleachers – home side
ii. Scoreboards do have horns – could move to other side so that the sound is
facing the school, rather than the neighbors
iii. Storage building
iv. Future items: visitor bleachers, ticket booths, concession building: would
go back to project review board for an addition when needed.
v. Submit and get approval
vi. P.A. system will need to be addressed. Attendance numbers will help with
estimation. Acoustic consultant will be helpful with this. These may need
to be mounted on their own poles on school side poles pointing back.

Consultant said they could be on existing poles. Need sound so
participants on tracks can hear upcoming events.
1. In soccer, horn is blown when there needs to be substitutions.
g. Other issues to consider:
i. May need to be another vehicle access for emergency. Would enter by
Morse Street.
ii. Landscape on north side; Pat will need to work with the neighbors. Also,
having property line remarked. Surveyor will be out next week. Thinking
we will put in a hedge of large shrubs. Could also look at fast growing
pines. May bring in an arborist.
iii. Trying to be on March agenda.
iv. Athletic lighting ordinance will be done late.
v. Work on the guidelines
1. Estimates of attendance from our lights/night game – were 500
2. Could look at adding some Saturday day games to lessen night
games.
3. Yarmouth played 19 games at night this past fall
4. Kim, Tim, and Craig will work on the revision
vi. Traffic and Parking:
1. Not under Project Review Board purview
2. But would like us to go to them.
3. We do have a traffic study. We have a parking map.
4. Contact at L.L. Bean – Don Forster
5. Ordinance does not allow us to put up temporary signs
6. Maps/brochures issued to visiting teams
7. Can we add on to L.L. Bean signs?
8. If night games, may need some type of pedestrian lighting.
vii. Discussed revisions to the design
1. Added another triple jump/long jump
2. Can’t put it inside the track due to the width of our design
viii. Feb. 17th – next meeting at 7:30 in Room 102
Action Steps:
a. Craig, Kim, Tim work on guidelines
b. Andrew/Pat Revised plan to town by next Wednesday March 1st agenda

Track and Field Neighborhood Meeting
Thursday, February 2, 2017
6:30 p.m.
Minutes
Attendance: John Simoneau, Becky Foley, Michelle Ritcheson, Kim LaMarre, Andrew Johnston,
Pat Carroll, Craig Sickels; Residents of the Neighborhood
John Simoneau, Chair of the Track and Field Committee, presented an overview of the project.
Most of the meeting was spent hearing concerns and questions from the neighborhood.
Concerns that the Neighborhood shared:
PA System:
• plan for the PA system:
• the noise from the system
• how often will it be utilized
• how late will it be on
Fireworks
Ongoing communication:
• Ongoing communications – keeping neighbors Informed about the big events
• Ensuring that not too many big events are taking place at the same time and at
the busiest times of year; Hunter Road Fields has a limit on events.
• Concerned about the messaging to staff, students, bus drivers about driving
safely. How will this message be conveyed?
• Ensuring there is communication between town, school, and neighborhood.
Lights:
• The amount of light usage.
Parking:
• Parking at Snow Dump
• Morse Street Parking Lot – not allowing students to park there.
• Is there adequate parking for the events?
Access:
• Increase access for the pedestrians from LL Bean lot.
• Will Snow Road be closed during construction?
• Can more definitive pathways be created from parking lot to fields.
Landscaping:
• Neighbors would love additional screening through landscaping.
Signage:
• Signage for events to divert them to the correct parking lots.

Adequate Restroom Facilities:
• Ensuring there are enough facilities for events
• Trash/littering.
Law Enforcement:
• Will there be an increased law enforcement presence?
• Concerns about buses, students, spectators following the speed limit
Next Steps:
Feb. 8 – Concept Plan presented to the Project Review Board
March 15 - Review Meeting at the Town
Submission Requirement: could possibly be a 3rd meeting.

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/7650f581-d6f5-425d-88aa-a77e781d5b61
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/other-committees/diversity-equity-and-inclusion
TITLE: February 5, 2026 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Tom Gray, Superintendent of Schools Erica Mazzeo, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources Elisha Morris, Director of Instructional Support
Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Belonging Committee
February 5, 2026
Meeting via Google Meet
Minutes
Committee Members: Ashley Schumacher, Valerie Williams, Malik Farlow, Erica Mazzeo, Jen
Gulko, Tom Gray,
Guests Present: Hadley Herling (Student); Shelby Jenusaitis (Student); Lexi Triggiani, School
Counselor, DCS; Pam Crosby, Principal, FMS; Kelsi Gagne, parent (MSS)
Agreed upon Norms
● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing
judgment.
● Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to seek
accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times. Intentions
do not negate impact.
● Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we work.
● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and encouraging
each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and avoid the
discomfort around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the
topic at hand.
Adopted 11/14/23
DEI[B] Committee Charge: The DEI[B] Committee is charged with creating a culture in RSU
No. 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are respected and celebrated through the
implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative strategies that inspire an inclusive
mindset in RSU No. 5 employees, students, and community members.
17 West Street Freeport, ME 04032 · 207-865-0928

To better execute this charge, the Committee had adopted the following definitions (Guiding
Definitions HERE) of the terms core to our work. These definitions have been adapted from
resources developed by a range of secondary and higher education institutions who have
researched these domains.
Agenda Items and Discussion:
1. Share Introductions - Committee members and guests introduced themselves
2. 10 minutes Jigsaw Guiding Definitions - How can we explain these guiding terms more
succinctly?
Diversity - Awareness of all the different types of diverse humans in the world
Equity - Recognizing & Responding to individual needs so that every person has access to
opportunities and support required to succeed.
Inclusion - Embracing differences and eliminating barriers
Belonging - Support and Connection - Feeling included by showing your true authentic self
and experiencing support and connection.
3. Solicit Feedback from committee members regarding District Strategic Plan goals, other
work and needs
Committee members and guests shared the following:
● Doing more work for parents, especially those whose children have
disabilities/individual needs, celebrate differences, elevate awareness
● How do we stay committed to this work while federal funding is being
threatened/pulled. How do we build these things in for the long term/build things out
meaningfully?
○ Recognizing places where institutional oppression is playing out. Ex: When
we’re forced to consider cuts understanding how we compensate for those
most marginalized populations
● Safety for families in our community
● Time to share ‘the work’ in the committee has been valuable. Going forward, what is
the action this team takes? What is the scope of this team’s work?
● Would like to see the committee continue to focus on the curriculum - civics ed,
elevating the voices of marginalized populations.
● Support for students with disabilities - parents, students, and staff - more global
understanding
○ Understanding of the intention of the DEIB group
Next meeting: Focus on the action of the committee

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Tom Gray, Superintendent of Schools Erica Mazzeo, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources Elisha Morris, Director of Instructional Support
Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Belonging Committee
Agenda
February 5, 2026
10:30 a.m.
Video call link: https://meet.google.com/hjm-cimf-bfa
Or dial: (US) +1 478-308-5304 PIN: 558 066 662#
Committee Members: Ashley Schumacher, Valerie Williams, Malik Farlow, Erica Mazzeo, Jen Gulko,
Tom Gray
Agreed upon Norms
● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing judgment.
● Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to seek
accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times. Intentions do
not negate impact.
● Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we work.
● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and encouraging each
other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and avoid the discomfort
around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the topic at
hand.
Adopted 11/14/23
DEI[B] Committee Charge: The DEI[B] Committee is charged with creating a culture in RSU No. 5
where diversity, equity, and inclusion are respected and celebrated through the implementation of
thoughtful, iterative, and innovative strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU No. 5
employees, students, and community members.
To better execute this charge, the Committee had adopted the following definitions (Guiding Definitions
HERE) of the terms core to our work. These definitions have been adapted from resources developed
by a range of secondary and higher education institutions who have researched these domains.
17 West Street Freeport, ME 04032 · 207-865-0928

Agenda Items and Discussion:
1. Share Introductions
2. 10 minutes Jigsaw Guiding Definitions
3. Solicit Feedback from committee members regarding District Strategic Plan goals, other work
and needs

thoughtful, iterative, and innovative strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU No. 5

HERE) of the terms core to our work. These definitions have been adapted from resources developed
by a range of secondary and higher education institutions who have researched these domains.
____________________________________________________________________________________
Agenda Items and Discussion:
1. Reflect on the work of the District DEI[B] Committee to date
● All members asked to share historical perspectives of committee work.
● DEIB Committee Charge statement is now adjusted to reflect the changes made in 2024
● DEIB Definitions (also 2024) now attached (adjustment)
● Members shared historical information about the formation of the committee,
composition of membership, efforts to include student membership, professional
development, and document creation
● Jen shared high school specific efforts including goals within the high school’s strategic
plan and guiding documents (in shared drive)
● Erica shared Districtwide PD opportunities thus far this year including Counted Out
Screening, Choosing to See Book Study, Wabanaki Educator’s Conference, and
Dawnland Screening. And, likely future work: Universal Design for Learning (UDL)
discussions, Grading Discussions, etc.
2. Project what is needed next
● Determining what DEIB work we want represented in the District’s Strategic Plan
● Promote Student Engagement. District will identify students for committee participation -
Remote attendance, supported by school administrators
● Meeting times (Thursdays @ 10:30): February 5, April 2, June 4, 2026)
○ Share Introductions
○ 10 minutes Jigsaw Guiding Definitions
○ Solicit Feedback from committee members regarding District Strategic Plan
goals, other work and needs

17WestStreetFreeport,ME04032· 207-865-0928

Definitions for Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Belonging
RSU No. 5 DEI Committee
(Developed 2024)
The DEI Committee is charged with creating a culture in RSU No. 5 where diversity, equity, and
inclusion are respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and
innovative strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU No. 5 employees, students, and
community members.
To better execute this charge, the Committee had adopted the following definitions of the terms
core to our work. These definitions have been adapted from resources developed by a range of
secondary and higher education institutions who have researched these domains..
A Note on Integration and Evolution:
Equity and Belonging cannot be achieved without the foundation of Diversity and the actions of
Inclusion. The four are intimately linked and necessary for a full realization of the potential of
any group and organization. Each is in relation to individuals, small and large groups, informal
and formal collectives, organizations and institutions. The four are also in constant flux as
society and the academy discover and learn from our history and our complex present.
Definitions will continue to evolve while the motivation for a just and humane society will remain
unchanged.1
Diversity
Diversity refers to the variety of personal experiences, values, and worldviews that arise from
differences of culture and circumstance. Such differences include race, ethnicity, gender and
gender identity, age, religion, language, disability status, sexual orientation, socioeconomic
status, geographic region, and more.2
Equity
Equity refers to actively working to identify and eliminate barriers that have prevented full
participation across differences in culture and circumstance, specifically redressing the
exclusion of historically underrepresented groups in higher education.3 Equitable environments
1MassachusettsInstituteofTechnology(“Diversity,Equity,andInclusionResources,”
https://hr.mit.edu/diversity-equity-inclusion/resources)
2RutgersCollege(“DEICommitteeGoodStartGuide,”
https://nbdiversity.rutgers.edu/dei-committee-good-start-guide)
3Rutgers

address disparities and strive to eliminate unique and systemic barriers that have prevented the
full participation of marginalized groups. Such environments are free from discrimination,
harassment and bias.4
Inclusion
Inclusion refers to the act of creating environments in which individuals and groups feel
welcomed, respected, supported, and valued, by eliminating practices and behaviors that
marginalize. An inclusive climate embraces differences and offers respect in words and actions
so that all people can fully participate in the university’s opportunities.5 Such actions involve
intentional and ongoing engagement with the diversity in and across various social, cultural, and
intellectual groups. Inclusive environments increase productivity, awareness, knowledge, and
empathic understanding of the complex ways individuals interact within groups. Inclusion is
distinct from belonging and strives for belonging.6
Belonging
Belonging refers to the emotional and experiential outcome of inclusion.7 Belonging is the
feeling individuals, particularly those from historically underrepresented groups who have
experienced exclusion in the past, have in places that have intentionally actionized diversity,
equity, and inclusion to create a culture that supports the feeling of security, acceptance, and
value.8 Belonging is the sense that you can bring your full and authentic self to a group.9
Belonging gives people a voice in a community, and encourages and enables them to contribute
to it and to expect support from it as well.10
4MIT
5Rutgers
6MIT
7NationalAssociationofIndependentSchools(“Diversity,Equity,Inclusion,andBelonging,”
https://www.nais.org/articles/pages/diversity-equity-inclusion-and-belonging/)
8AssociationofPublicandLand-GrantUniversities,(“APLU’sDiversity,Equity,Inclusion,&Belonging
(DEIB)Definitions,”https://www.aplu.org/wp-content/uploads/aplu-deib-definitions.pdf)
9MIT
10NAIS

judgment.

seek accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times.
Intentions do not negate impact.
● Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we
work.
● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and
encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and
avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the
topic at hand.

Submitted By: Jean Skorapa, Superintendent
Committee Members in Attendance: Michelle Tussing, Lily West, Malik Farlow, Colin Cheney, Jen
Gulko, Cynthia Alexander, June Sellers, Jean Skorapa
Members absent: Allie Edwards
Agreed upon Norms
● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing
judgment.
● Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to
seek accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times.

Adopted 11/14/23
RSU 5 Strategic Plan Goal: Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.
DEI Committee Charge: Create a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are
respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative
strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community
members.
Agenda Items and Discussion:
17WestStreetFreeport,ME04032· 207-865-0928

1. DEIB - Establishment of Common Definitions
a. The committee reviewed a compilation of definitions of diversity, equity, inclusion,
and belonging. The committee discussed how a common understanding of the
terms will support the development of actionable steps.
b. The information will be shared with Freeport High School’s Equity Team and the
SAF (Student Action in Freeport) Group for input regarding current practices at
the school
c. Feedback from other schools will be solicited in the future.
2. Procedures for Displaying Flags
a. The committee discussed the school’s obligation to create a safe learning
environment for all students and also honoring the right to free speech.
b. Principal Gulko will follow up with the committee with input from other principals
regarding the establishment of procedures.
3. Committee Membership
a. The committee will review committee membership and the charge of the
committee at the next meeting.
4. Upcoming Meetings - via ZOOM
February 13, 2024
March 12, 2024
April 9, 2024
May 14, 2024

Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Committee
January 9, 2024
7:00 a.m.
https://networkmaine.zoom.us/j/83955767193
Meeting ID: 839 5576 7193
Committee Members: Allie Edwards, Michelle Tussing Lily West, Colin Cheney, Malik Farlow,
Cynthia Alexander, Jen Gulko, June Sellers, Jean Skorapa
Agreed upon Norms
● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing
judgment.
● Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to
seek accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times.
Intentions do not negate impact.
● Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we
work.
● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and
encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and
avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the
topic at hand.
Adopted 11/14/23

DEI Committee Charge: Create a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are
respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative
strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community
members.

“Toinspireandsupporteverylearnerbychallengingminds,buildingcharacter,sparkingcreativity,andnurturingpassions.”
JeanSkorapa,SuperintendentofSchools CynthiaAlexander,AssistantSuperintendentofSchools
KellyWentworth,DirectorofFinance&HumanResources JuneSellers,Ed.D.,DirectorofInstructionalSupport
Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Committee
December 12, 2023
Minutes
Submitted By: Jean Skorapa, Superintendent
Committee Members in Attendance: Michelle Tussing, Lily West, Malik Farlow, Colin Cheney, Jen
Gulko, June Sellers, Jean Skorapa
Members absent: Allie Edwards, Cynthia Alexander,
Agreed upon Norms
● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing
judgment.
● Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to
seek accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times.
Intentions do not negate impact.
● Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we
work.
● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and
encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and
avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the
topic at hand.
Adopted 11/14/23
RSU 5 Strategic Plan Goal: Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.
DEI Committee Charge: Create a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are
respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative
strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community
members.
Agenda Items and Discussion:
1. DEI “B” - Belonging Discussion
17WestStreetFreeport,ME04032· 207-865-0928

a. The committee reviewed several definitions of diversity, equity, inclusion and
belonging and discussed the importance of integrating terms. A common
understanding of the terms will support the development of actionable steps.
The committee will continue discussion at the next meeting.
2. Displaying of Flags
a. It was recommended that a procedure is established for the displaying of items.
School walls are not public forums.
b. Principal Gulko will reach out to fellow principals regarding their established
procedures and bring information back to the committee in January.
3. Committee Membership
a. Membership requirements were established by the board when the DEI
Committee was an Advisory Committee. Now that the committee is an “other”
committee further discussion needs to take place regarding committee makeup
and how membership is determined.
4. Upcoming Meetings - via ZOOM
January 9, 2024
February 13, 2024
March 12, 2024
April 9, 2024
May 14, 2024

DEI Committee Charge: with creating a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community members. 
Agenda
1. Housing Impact on Immigrant Students & Families - June
   1. As of Monday, 22 New Mainers remain enrolled.  This number is down from 66 students in October.  RSU5 will continue to provide transportation through the end of the school year.
   2. We believe Casco Bay Inn families can stay until they find permanent housing.
   3. Families in South Portland have been told that they must vacate their hotel by June 30th.


2. ACAAA - Transgender and Gender Expansive Students - Cynthia
   1. One final editing meeting will be held by the Policy Committee next week based on feedback from the district’s attorneys.
   2. The Committee reviewed the proposed policy as written.
   3. PowerSchool - Could preferred pronouns be added to a field to support students? 
   4. The Committee suggested sharing statistics with the board when presenting the policy.
   5. State law regarding bathroom use is approximately 10 years old.


3. Book Challenge Process - Jean (TABLED until next meeting)
   1. IJJ - Instructional and Library-Media Materials Selection
   2. IJJ-E Citizen’s Challenge of Educational Media Form

Agenda




RSU 5 Strategic Plan Goal:  Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.


DEI Committee Charge: with creating a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community members. 


1. Housing Impact on Immigrant Students & Families - June
   1. Several families have permanent housing.
   2. Multilingual learners
      1. 66 currents multilingual learners in the the district; 36 of these are New Mainers
2. Unpacking School Culture & Climate
   1. While school climate and culture have been defined in a number of ways, and at times are used interchangeably, they are two distinct terms which overlap and influence one another:
      1. School climate refers to the individual experiences and feelings that students, teachers, and staff have about the school; it’s the feel of the school (the schools’ attitude), and how people feel within the school, including the behaviors and points of view exhibited and experienced by students, teachers and other stakeholders.
      2. School culture typically refers to the long-term physical and social environment, as well as the values; it’s how schools do things; how the school, students, teachers and families work together and the set of values, beliefs, and assumptions they share.
3. Review submitted ideas: DEI District Theory of Action
   1. Committee members were asked to complete the survey prior to our May meeting.
   2. Submissions by Committee Members:
      1. A curriculum that represents diverse perspectives, staff professional development for supporting inclusivity and equity; protocols that ensure equal access to school programs (e.g. financial support to remove barriers & ensure equity of access to school programming & opportunities), expansion of student leadership opportunities
      2. Develop and implement anti-racist practices for classrooms for all age levels; Develop a glossary of terms around anti-racism and inclusion for use across RSU; Support (through funding and designated time) yearly anti-racist training for educators, administrators, and other staff; Fund-raise in the community for visiting writers/speakers/artists to present on anti-racist and LGBTQ-supportive issues; Create opportunities for the community to experience work being done in the schools around these issues.
      3. Include parents/families in learning and dialogue in regard to this work 
4. Committee Roles
   1. Standing Committees are permanent committees established by the Board of Directors.  All standing committee meetings are open to the public. These committees facilitate district governance and address ongoing needs. All standing committees are comprised of less than a majority of the Board of Directors. Standing committees may research issues and make recommendations for Board action but may not act for the Board. The RSU5 Board of Directors has the following standing committees: finance, policy, strategic communications.
   2. Advisory Committees perform specific functions for the Board of Directors. The committees may study particular problems or issues and make reports and/or recommendations to the Board but may not act for the Board. Each advisory committee shall have at least one Board member. An advisory committee shall automatically dissolve after the Board has received the committee’s final report or recommendations. No advisory committee shall continue for a prolonged period without a specific assignment. 
   3. Other Committees: The superintendent may establish other committees as deemed necessary for the efficient operation of the District.
   4. Other, Other Committees (District)
      1. Safety Committee
      2. Wellness Committee
5. DEI Committee Membership, terms of appointment and service 
   1. How can we ensure that we have an equitable, transparent, and inclusive process?
   2. Jean, Cynthia & June will put together a proposal for committee sustainability
6.   For the Good of the Order
1. Continue to seek student feedback on the work of the committee, and clarity on the work & outcomes of the committee
2. What is the stance of the DEI committee? 
   1. Given that we now know the change in regard to the status of the committee, what is the charge of the committee moving forward?
3. Recommendation to do a case study of districts who are experiencing challenges to DEI initiatives?
7. Next meeting:
1. Jean will talk us through what would happen if we had a book challenge

RSU 5 Strategic Plan Goal:  Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.


DEI Committee Charge: with creating a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community members. 


1. Housing Impact on Immigrant Students & Families - June
   1. Our families can remain in the hotels in South Portland until the end of June.
   2. Transportation is being paid for with grant funding.
   3. Additional sheltering is currently available at the Portland Expo.
   4. Needed Resources:  
      1. Securing transportation to athletic/co-curricular activities has been challenging.
   5. FY 24 Budgetary Considerations
      1. Transportation costs were added to our budget because the grant ends on Sept 30th.  
      2. An additional ESOL teacher was added to the budget.


2. The committee reviewed the following resource:  The Maine Department of Education’s Commitment and Support for Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion in Maine schools  
   1. Includes the commitment to support:
      1. Every educator in every role shares the responsibility for ensuring equity for every student and participates in equity education, both in teacher and administrator preparation programs and ongoing throughout their careers.
      2. All academic and non-academic programming is culturally responsive and co-constructed with community members.
      3. Reviewing your SAU’s Controversial Issues (check out this resource for support) policy and best practices for engaging in discussions responsively and responsibly.
      4. Expecting all school personnel to engage in professional learning about anti-racism and culturally responsive practices.
      5. Reviewing and revising curricula and materials to ensure they are well-rounded, decolonized, and representing all experiences.
      6. Establishing expectations that every student will achieve and is challenged with rigorous curricula.
      7. Maine Department of Education statement regarding Our Commitment and Shared Resources to Combat Racism. 


3. Charge of DEI Committee
   1. Theory of Action - A theory of action is a hypothesis about what will happen when a set of strategies is implemented. Developing a theory of action requires using critical judgment about which strategic actions will lead to what desired results. It is the process of connecting what we plan to do with what we hope to get.




SENTENCE STEM:


IF we implement _(brief strategy description)_, including the following specific components ____(program elements) ___ THEN (circle all that apply) teachers/administrators/parents will ____(adult behaviors)____ and students will _________(student behaviors and outcomes)____. 


EXAMPLE: School Level


IF we implement strategies to improve the culture and climate of our school, including the following specific components restorative justice practices, and a new after school mentoring program for students with frequent discipline actions and absences THEN teachers and administrators will enhance their relationships with students and use more effective conflict resolution techniques, and students will feel more positively about their school experience and spend more time engaged in instruction. 


RSU5 DEI Committee Theory of Action
IF we implement thoughtful, iterative, and innovative strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5, including the following specific components ____(program elements) ___ THEN RSU5 employees, students, and community members will transform our community into a place where each individual will thrive and grow as they are recognized for their inherent value in an evolving world.


4. DEI District Theory of Action  
   1. Committee members are asked to complete the survey prior to the May meeting.


5. Agenda Building
   1. Theory of Action
   2. Membership
