Agenda


Strategic Plan Goal:  Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.


Attendance: Erin Abbott, Jen Gulko, Michelle Tussing, Colin Cheney, Susana Hancock, Jean Skorapa, Cynthia Alexander, June Sellers, Lily West, Allie Edwards, Katie Roy




Absent:  Julie Labbay, Karen Breer, Victoria Winters 
 
   1. Housing Impact on Students - June
   1. At the end of February some families may have no housing.
   2. Multilingual Learners - 45 students currently.


   2. Equity Work Feedback Survey Data - Cynthia
   1. 54 individuals participated in a survey approximately a year ago.
 Forms response chart. Question title: 1. What professional development did you participate in regarding diversity, equity, and inclusion?  (Check all  that apply.)

. Number of responses: 54 responses. 

2. What strategy or strategies have you implemented in your department, building, classroom, or school environment based on what you have learned?
   * I am more aware of and careful to adhere to preferred pronouns.
   * n/a
   * gender neutral phrasing, unit prep
   * We watched The Danger of a Single Story in my reading classes so students are prepared to approach learning tools, resources, and life through an equitable lens.
   * We just started the book study so I have not walked away with any strategies to implement yet.
   * I have changed names in math word problems to be more inclusive, use gender neutral pronouns, say students instead of boys and girls, and just generally encourage acceptance all around and redirect students to show acceptance. Lots of small changes but important.
   * I've been including information on important mathematicians that are not featured in their textbooks (females, those of color, etc); not giving assignments that require certain tools and resources (e.g. rulers); meeting with kids who don't have the opportunity to meet with me for help during advisory at the very end of the day or early before school starts
   * As a school leader, I have facilitated staff professional learning and discussions. The school has selected a Equity Walkthrough tool to use as well.
   * "Culture Corner" starting with Culture Flipbooks and then kids signing up to share something from their culture; incorporating language of windows, mirrors and sliding doors; more purposeful selection of culturally diverse books in classroom library and read-alouds
   * Offering independent choice reading, teaching the relevance of Jim Crow racism as similar issues apply to our current world, inviting students to share information about their names and their backgrounds, but many of these are not new strategies for me.
   * In advisory, we did an activity where we reflected on our public vs. private face, then discussed our findings in general terms; also, in Endeavor, we plan field trips that expose students to experiences they otherwise would not have the opportunity to have (socioeconomic equity)
   * The resources we use are from a wider spectrum of voices.
   * Being more intentional with diversity in read-alouds and the classroom library
   * Working on remaking the SS units
   * I introduce a different artist every single day to my students. I make sure to include a wide variety of artists (people of color, LGBTQ+, disabled, etc.). My classroom is covered with diverse images (portraits and artwork).
   * I can't say I have implemented any new strategies since this training began but I would like to return to the MAEC work which included teaching students how to disrupt bullying that can be rooted in class, race, gender, or other identity based judgments.
   * The trainings helped as we have begun to have New Mainers enrolling, I have used information in my conversations with families and students learned from an Equity Maine presentation at school
   * Checked my book offerings for "mirrors & windows", changed my language around "boys & girls" to "friends", etc.
   * "windows and mirrors" in classroom library, discussing difficult topics with kids
   * I have focused more on inclusive language.
   * Read-alouds of books and discussion about race, racism, and skin color
   * I have diversified my classroom library to include quality multicultural literature.
   * Audited materials and resources for inclusivity (making adjustments as needed).
   * I am generally more aware of my language in regards to what is considered “normal”
   * Dialed back expectations of work done outside of school due to the variety of (lack of, on some cases) at home resources/support for outside work.
   * I am much more aware of my privilege (white, male, middle class) and my biases and much more.
   * Book choices, and changes to Social Studies representing Maine indigenous peoples.
   * Google Translate, visual supports
   * I am not sure if this fits the question, because I am not sure if either of these came from the activities I participated in. But I am reading Grading For Equity right now and thinking about how I can make small shifts in my grading, and also how we might start to talk about school-wide practices. I have also shifted to a different textbook that features a more diverse cast of characters and less colonialist perspective. I think the PD I participated in just maybe raised my awareness and made it seem like change is possible.
   * I've continued to work on building a diverse library and considering DEIJ when selecting class read-alouds. DEIJ has also been a consistent lens while developing SS curriculum, infusing our discussions and influencing our decisions.
   * Student identity mapping


   * Differentiation, Responsive Classroom
   * Remembering to keep an open mind when working with all people and everyone has a background that influences their responses, reactions, and needs to be held up with care.
   * We rotate between science and social studies units, and have been in a science unit, so nothing yet.
   * Something I have thought about based on what I have learned in staff meetings is the idea of "chance versus design." The things in my classroom that happen because I have taught them or the things that happen by luck. This has helped me zoom into the skills I want students developing over a two year period and it has opened my eyes to the things I need to take more time to teach into.
   * None specifically so far
   * I made some changes to what I teach (books), as well as changes to how I teach them. We also read and discussed these topics through professional books. Part of this work also validated the curriculum we were already teaching.
   * Building psychological safety as a foundation of this work
   * I guess I'm not sure what you mean by strategies - I try to employ an equity lens in all that I do. Having time to reflect on everything that we do, everyday, with our learners and employing that lens is important and what we should all strive for. One area I've been thinking about this fall, and have discussed with our third grade team, is how we teach Freeport history. We need time to really learn the history of Freeport, and not just from the white settlers' perspective.
   * Our social studies curriculum has been changed to incorporate these ideas and principles. Additionally, as an educator, I've become more mindful of this in my day-to-day work.
   * None
   * None
   * I try to make my room a safe space where all students are accepted.
   * I work hard to provide an environment conducive to including all students.
   * Music literature review and broadening of repertoire
   * Provided district-wide PLD for teachers PK-5, focusing on differentiation as an equity issue; disaggregating math data by race/language status/gender; supporting teachers in grouping students heterogeneously in math to support inclusion of all learners; currently reading the book "Coaching for Equity" to improve my professional knowledge.
   * Building classroom library with books to promote equity and diversity
   * Shift in language
   * Social Studies work, diverse library, language / how to interrupt, educate, etc. student conversation to promote inclusiveness
   * Assessed and modified current curriculum to include more equity.
   * We restructured our hiring practice. We made all meetings trauma sensitive. We looked at all of the language on our forms and opted to release all binary language around gender. We selected a consulting agency to guide our DEI work as it was irresponsible to do it on our own.
   * Greater representation of diversity in framing tasks and challenges for my students.
   * Created an equity team, created a student leadership team, helped student leaders facilitate student-led discussions about equity, led a book study on Grading for Equity, implemented advisory lessons about respecting diverse identities
   * Developing thoughtful lessons- ordering resources that reflect our work
   * Using conversation to call people "in" rather than "out"
   * Engagement with parents about equity and inclusion picture books for read-alouds
   * 1. Equitable names and building respect around that.
   * 2. Advisory activities on respect.
   * 3. Education and discussions on appropriateness of student activities
   * Classroom strategies that include all.
   * More diversity in book selections, discussions on equity during units on environmental issues, as well as others.
   * I have supported others PD for this by finding guest teachers, and have supported these efforts in my communication to staff, students, and the community. I would have loved the opportunity to participate in more learning for this.


 Forms response chart. Question title: 3. Please select your role from the list below.. Number of responses: 54 responses. 

  



   3. Grading for Equity - Freeport High School
   1. Staff members are involved in discussing equitable practices for grading Habits of Work (HOW) collectively and by department.  The goal is for all kids to have the opportunity to be successful, regardless of situation.
   4. Colin- curious about the “equity” term. Are we looking at sub-groups?
   1. YES.
   1. FHS - Equity team- use data to drive focus. Includes discussions.
   5. Diversity is a fact; Equity is a Choice; Inclusion is an action; Belonging is an outcome.

Agreed upon Norms:
   * Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing judgment.
   * Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to seek accurate information. 
   * Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times. Intentions do not negate impact. 
   * Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we work.
   * Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations. 
   * Centering the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the topic at hand.

Agenda


Strategic Plan Goal:  Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.


Attendance: Allie Edwards, Erin Abbott, Katie Roy, Cynthia Alexander, June Sellers, Jen Gulko, Michelle Tussing, Karen Breer, Susana Hancock


Absent: Julie Labbay, Colin Cheney, Victoria Winters, Katie Roy


Guest: 
Resource: Equity Presentation- June 2022
   1. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium (MAEC) Report - Findings Review (pages 26-27) MAEC Report
   1. Note: An in-depth presentation was provided to this committee on January 12, 2021 where Karmen Rouland reviewed the entire report including the findings. 
   2. MAEC’s work with the district included:
   1. Review of policies in depth with eye for equity
   2. Conducted focus groups
   3. Provided professional development at board level
   4. Presented findings to the board and answered questions
   5. Professional development for entire district at a March professional development day regarding equity; provided work in break out groups
   6. Professional development for directors and their departments
   2. Each school developed equity goals
   3. CORTICO presentation from the high school in June; work with high school leadership continues
   4. Recommendations
   1. Create an Equity Task Force:   RSU 5 formed the DEI Committee.
   2. Support student-led initiatives:
   1. FHS student leadership team continues work with CORTIGO.  Student-led discussions will occur in February.  Areas of focus:  microaggressions, mental health, and school policies and procedures. 
   2. Each school has an active Civil Rights Team.
   3. Equity Officer - Each administrator and director is responsible for promoting equity and diversity throughout their school/department.
   4. Develop Strategic Plan for Equity and Diversity:  An equity goal has been added to RSU 5’s strategic plan.
   5. Adopt Policy Changes:  Policies were reviewed by Drummond/Woodsum, reviewed by the policy committee and adopted by the board.
   6. Professional Development:  Specific work has been completed
   1. In every professional development opportunity, the district infuses concepts of equity.  There are standards in the Teacher Evaluation rubric that address this concept.
   2. This year FHS is working on grading for equity.
   3. FHS student leadership team provided professional development for teachers regarding the use of pronouns.
   7. Periodic community forums
   1. DCS did a great deal of work with families last year.
   2. FHS recently did a survey regarding AP classes.
   3. Parent satisfaction survey is a conduit for feedback.
   4. An in-person forum may be helpful to reach other groups.
   8. Engage in authentic, culturally responsive family engagement practices:  language use takes time.
   9. Review the PK-12 curriculum to ensure that it is culturally responsive and representative of the student body:  This work is ongoing.  Currently the work is focused on social studies.
   10. Strengthen and sustain support for families in need.
   1. Each school provides specific support to students in need (ex. clothing, food, etc.)
   2. June works specifically w/principal for families experiencing homelessness.
   3. The district works with Freeport Community Services to support the needs of New Mainers.
   11. Provide clear and transparent communication to families regarding COVID-19:  The district followed CDC guidelines.  Mandatory pieces were removed in March, but individual choice (ex. masking) honored.
   5. Discussion:  How do we remain organized for this work as an administrative team? 
   6. Agenda Setting:
   1. Update - Housing:  Impact of Students

Agenda


Strategic Plan Goal:  Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.

Guest: Ryun


   1. Welcome New Member:  Dr. June Sellers
   1. Jean introduced June, Director of Instructional Support


   7. Draft Policy ACAAA:  Transgender and Gender Expansive Student
   1. Recent communication to staff “From Guidelines to Policy”


   2. The Policy Committee needs to consider that any substantive changes we make may unintentionally alter the law and need to be run by our counsel.


   3. Freeport High School’s Civil Rights Team and the GSA Team provided feedback regarding the policy  Student Feedback
   1. B.  Definitions
4.  Gender expansive – An umbrella term used to describe a person who expands notions of gender expression and identity beyond perceived or expected societal gender norms/binary.
   2. C.  Addressing the Needs of Transgender and Gender Expansive Students
2.  If requested by the student, or if deemed necessary by the administration to address school-related issues, a meeting will be scheduled to discuss the student’s particular circumstances and needs.
3. If there is an agreement to notify the parents between the stuent and administration, The student will be notified by the administrator prior to contacting their parent(s)/guardian(s).
   3. D.  Guidance on Specific Issues
3.  Names/Pronouns:  The student should be addressed by school staff, substitutes, volunteers and other students, by the name and pronoun corresponding to their gender identity that is asserted expressed at school. 
7.  Dress Code:  The student is expected to comply with Board policies and school rules regardless of gender identity.

Agenda


Attendance: Becky, Cynthia, Katie, Erin, Karen, Colin, Michelle, Riley, Jen, Susana, Allie


   1. Work with Cortico - Jen
   1. Jen Chase will be meeting with Becky and Jen on April 25th along with several of the students.
   2. Students will present to Becky and Jen on Apri 28 at 9:30
   2. Cultural appropriation - how do we define it? - Jen/Becky
   1. Dilemma-Senior Project
   1. Proposals in February. Time at the end of the year to complete the proposal. They have an advisor and sometimes they have a field advisor. There is a presentation element. 
   2. It has to have a community give-back or a social justice component or academic learning.
   2. This year- question on whether it's cultural appropriation or not. We feel it is. The field advisor is dead set that it is not. I reread the chapter on cultural appropriation. 
   1. The Parents asked if we have a definition of this as a district. I said we do not.
   2. I am bringing it to this team. Do we need a definition?
   3. We did give guidance for the project. They must include a member of that culture as an advisor in their project, so that part has been addressed.
   3. Definition to ponder:
   1. Jen: “We can broadly define cultural appropriation as the adoption or exploitation of another culture  by a more dominant culture.” -
   2. Karen: the unacknowledged or inappropriate adoption of the customs, practices, ideas, etc. of one people or society by members of another and typically more dominant people or society.
   3. Karen: Defined as the use of a culture's symbols, artifacts, genres, rituals, or technologies by members of another culture, cultural appropriation can be placed into 4 categories: exchange, dominance, exploitation, and transculturation.
   4. Karen: Cultural appropriation refers to the use of objects or elements of a non-dominant culture in a way that reinforces stereotypes or contributes to oppression and doesn't respect their original meaning or give credit to their source. It also includes the unauthorized use of parts of their culture (their dress, dance, etc.) without permission.  
   5. https://www.verywellmind.com/what-is-cultural-appropriation-5070458
   4. Michelle: Are we looking to create one or adopt a definition or are we just looking to point to one?  I would prefer the latter.  Colin agrees.  Becky would like a working definition for staff to gain learning around it.
   5. Important to acknowledge when using secondary sources/ biases and provide analysis. Important to seek out primary sources.
   6. Katie and Riley  (student) have not learned about cultural appropriation in school.  Learned about it through social media. Riley feels that what she did as a young student was not appropriate and it is important to learn about this in school. 
   7. Jen: adults don’t even understand cultural appropriation.  E.g. Hawaiian Luau 
   8. Susana: How would your actions be perceived by other cultures?
   9. Include throughout the curriculum as appropriate. Why is it relevant in research? What sources have authors used?


How to Know If Something Is Cultural Appropriation
If you are unsure how to decide if something is cultural appropriation, here are some questions to ask yourself:
   * What is your goal with what you are doing?
   * Are you following a trend or exploring the history of a culture?
   * Are you deliberately trying to insult someone's culture or are you being respectful?
   * Are you purchasing something (e.g., artwork) that is a reproduction of a culture or an original?
   * How would people from the culture you are borrowing from feel about what you are doing?
   * Are there any stereotypes involved in what you are doing?
   * Are you using a sacred item (e.g., headdress) in a flippant or fun way?
   * Are you borrowing something from an ancient culture and pretending that it is new?
   * Are you crediting the source or inspiration of what you are doing?
   * If a person of the original culture were to do what you are doing, would they be viewed as "cool" or could they possibly face discrimination?
   * Are you wearing a costume (e.g., Geisha girl, tribal wear) that represents a culture?
   * Are you ignoring the cultural significance of something in favor of following a trend?
Explore these questions and always aim to show sensitivity when adopting elements from another culture.

   3. Questions focused on were:
   1. Pull out aspects of own identity and share one
   2. How does it impact others
   3. How does it impact other members of the FHS community
   4. Some shared religion, race, or other lighter topics

   1. November 1st.

We focus on habits of work.  Becky- Of the kids not eligible, what are the barriers? Some kids don’t try out because they are not eligible. DEI Committee
October 12, 2021
 7:00 am - 8:00 am
Join Zoom Meeting
https://networkmaine.zoom.us/j/83955767193
Meeting ID: 839 5576 7193


Committee Members: Allie Edwards, Erin Abbott, Jen Gulko, Julie Labbay, Katie West, Karen Breer, Michelle Tussing, Lindsey Furtney, Susana Hancock, Becky Foley, Cynthia Alexander, Victoria Winters, Katie Roy, Riley Simon
________________


                Agreed upon NORMS:
   * Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing judgment.
   * Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to seek accurate information. 
   * Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times. Intentions do not negate impact. 
   * Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we work.
   * Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations. 
   * Centering the participations or experiences of those most impacted by the topic at hand. 
________________

   1. Link to the zoom recording (only available for two weeks)
   2. Gave a lot of strategies for dealing with kids with anxiety
   3. Purpose is to normalize the anxiety
   4. Anxiety does not have to be a permanent condition
   5. Should not try and eliminate or avoid what causes the anxiety; instead teach the skills needed to deal with the anxiety
   6. Lynn made the anxiety more black and white - she names the anxiety. Helps to deal with an abstract concept
   7. Anxious Parent; Anxious Child is one of her books


   3. Integrative Inquiry - Jen

   * Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we work.
   * Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations. 
   * Centering the participations or experiences of those most impacted by the topic at hand.

   2. At the town level, the DEI Committee just started. The town of Freeport completed the 21-day challenge in January.  (podcasts/readings)
   3. Victoria is willing to share the resource list so that they can be shared with the administrators. Victoria will send it to alexanderc@rsu5.org.

   5. Strategic plan:  
   1. District Equity Goal - This goal is broad at this time. In August we will provide more details for the next year.  Jen added that each school also has additional goals that are more detailed.  We will link all of the building level equity goals.
   6. Policies-  Cynthia
   1. Sent several policies to MAEC in the fall. MAEC has reviewed and provided comments for the Policy Committee to consider. Sent five additional policies for review around Title IX. Regulations for Title IX changed in the past year. Drummond Woodsum have rewritten these policies, and they have been adopted by the Board. They are currently being used. The Policy Committee will begin with the student facing policies such as the expulsion policy and review. They will then be reviewed by the lawyers. This will take at least a year to review and revise. Some are simple upgrades; others will take more discussion. 
   2. All policies have now been reviewed in the past five years. 
   7. PD for all staff.

She has some interactive activities she will be using. The first goal is for us to recognize our implicit biases. There will also be breakout sessions for each group to discuss in smaller groups.

   *  Develop Working Norms  
   * Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing judgment.
   * Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to seek accurate information. 
   * Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times. Intentions do not negate impact. 
   * Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we work.
   * Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations. 
   * Centering the participations or experiences of those most impacted by the topic at hand. 
   *    *  Tri-town Equity Update - Victoria  
   *  Other updates 
   * March 12th professional development - Cynthia
   * Professional development day - contracting with MAEC - they will be providing pd over time. First day will be on Mar. 12 via zoom. 90 minutes for each session. All staff will attend. First initial will be focused on work thus far, summary of MAEC’s report, internal bias and some self-reflective work. What might be our own internal bias. Series over time. 
   * Civil Rights Teams - in the proposed budget
   * All schools will have stipend. DCS will 
   * At high school, this has evolved through student interest. Some years it has not run. Creating the stipend, will ensure that there will be a team every year at the high school. Trying to gather membership at the moment. Clubs meet during the day. Last week they showed the “I have a Dream” speech. Then had a discussion about the video. Small group currently.  Also been connecting with the Tri-town Equity Committee. 
When and how will conversations take place at the high school. Some of it is due to timing, due to open campus, due to feeling safe.
   * Future meeting times
   * This time works well for most of the adults in this group.
   * Students at the highschool would need a ride to school at this time.  Work and athletics may impact participation. High Schoolers may not love the 7AM time.  Adeline can do 4 PM
   * Backup time would be at 6:30 PM
   * We will continue next month at 7:00 AM and check in with Victoria to see about a switch to 6:30.

   * Intentionally engage with families that historically have been harder to reach, etc.   (to partner with families and build supports)

DEI Committee Agenda
12.8.20
7:00 am - 8:00 am
Join Zoom Meeting
https://networkmaine.zoom.us/j/83955767193
Meeting ID: 839 5576 7193
Join by telephone:   1 646 876 9923       




Committee Members: Allie Edwards, Erin Abbott, Jen Gulko, Julie Labbay, Katie West, Karen Breer, Michelle Tussing, Adeline Benica, TruHeart Peeples, Lindsey Furtney, Susana Hancock, Becky Foley, Cynthia Alexander, Victoria Winters


Attendance: Becky, Cynthia, Lindsey, Karen, Adeline, Susana, Jen, Erin, Julie, Katie, Victoria
Absent: Allie Edwards, TruHeart Peeples, Michelle Tussing




   5. Introductions:  Say your name, position and complete this sentence starter “I am……”   Example: I am Becky Foley, superintendent in RSU 5.  I am a quilter, a descendant of German grandparents, and a passionate educator.
   6. Purpose of the committee:
   1.  “Committee Charge:
   1. Whereas the RSU5 Board of Directors recognizes the importance of promoting practices that strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion within RSU 5, we hereby charge the RSU5 Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Advisory Committee (DEI) with creating a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community members. RSU 5 will strive to transform our community into a place where each individual will thrive and grow as they are recognized for their inherent value in an evolving world.”
   2. Decision-Making Considerations:
   1. The DEI will strive to build consensus among committee members when making recommendations to the RSU5 Board of Directors.  Final authority for all decisions relating to the equity plan rests with the RSU5 Board of Directors.
   3. Questions/Comments - none at this time
   7. Updates
   1. District Equity Goal - reviewed this goal. Waiting for the audit findings at the moment. 
   2. Cultural Competence Institute: 
   1. Ten staff enrolled: eight administrators and two staff members. Once a month from 6-7:30 pm we attend a Zoom event with 290 educators across the state with Lawrence Alexander as facilitator.  
   2. At each administrative meeting we bring our learning from CCI to the larger administrative team meeting for learning/discussion.
   3. All schools in RSU 5 have an equity goal. - many are waiting for audit findings. 
   4. Audit from MAEC: Mid-Atlantic Educational Consortium 
   1. Good turnout on the focus groups.
   2. MAEC will provide consultation and training. 
   3. Full report should be completed by the end of the week. Consultant is willing to come to our meetings to answer questions. 
   4. Will be making recommendations on certain policies. We have sent them the Title IX policy through the lens of equity. 
   5. Contract is a two year contract. Much of the professional development will begin next year or possibly in the spring. 
   5. Tri-Town Equity and Inclusion Committee - Victoria Winters 
   1. A. Founded by 8 people who reside in Freeport, but it is inclusive of Pownal and Durham. This was created after the Black Lives Matter protests this spring. 
   2. Purpose of the committee is to raise awareness to create an inclusive community for everyone. 
   3. Have sub-committees: Town Committee, RSU 5 committee. 
   4. Have met with all of the Freeport based schools, and reaching out to the other two schools. Want to be of service and to be a support. Civil Rights Teams have been a natural fit. 
   5. Community group - anti-racist challenge for 21 days. Curated by one of the parents. Begins January 17th. Can register through RSU 5 Community Programs. Connie Clifford is the point person on this. Middle school and high school are thinking of doing their own variety of this. Here is the link: https://rsu5cp.myrec.com/info/activities/program_details.aspx?ProgramID=30066
   6. Membership of the groups are growing. If you know of people interested, please contact Victoria Winters or Lindsey  Furtney. 
   6. At high school, have a Civil Rights Team - morphed into two different clubs. Looking at having students empowered but need some adult direction. Cumberland County principals meet on Fridays who have active Civil Rights Clubs. Some students perceive it as a club for  elementary students. They are looking at giving it a different name. 
   7. Questions/Comments
   8. Audit Findings Thus Far 
   1. Questions/Comments
   1. Lots of rich discussion among the committee about not being surprised that it was not recognized by students, but by parents. There is a need for professional development for staff in how to have difficult conversations. A book was recommended as a starting point: So You Want to Talk about Race. The high school staff just finished a book study on this book and found it helpful. There is a need to find specific professional development for communities that have the same demographics as ours. Also important to teach our students how to be allies to minority populations. 
   2. Full audit findings should be available by the next meeting. Will try and get it to committee members in advance so we can be prepared with questions. 
   9. Next Steps in Audit
   1. Meet with the Associate Director and sign agreements for data sharing
   1. E.g. De-identify where needed
   2. Collect and submit other data for review
   1. Discipline
   2. Survey data
   3. Attendance
   4. Climate data
   5. Achievement data (E.g. Scorecards)
   3. Meet with consultant in late December to determine next steps
   4. Assistant Superintendent Cynthia Alexander submitted student-facing policies as part of the audit and will meet with MAEC to review annotations from policies in January/February
   5. Questions, Comments
   10. Next meeting: 
   1. Time: January 12, 2021 7-8 am
   2. Agenda Items
   1. Consultant will review audit findings with group
   2. Updates
   1. Tri-town Equity
   2. Other updates

============================================================

“Toinspireandsupporteverylearnerbychallengingminds,buildingcharacter,sparkingcreativity,andnurturingpassions.”
JeanSkorapa,SuperintendentofSchools CynthiaAlexander,AssistantSuperintendentofSchools
KellyWentworth,DirectorofFinance&HumanResources JuneSellers,Ed.D.,DirectorofInstructionalSupport
Curriculumand ProgramDevelopment CommitteeReport
Committee: Curriculum and Program Development
Meeting date: April 9, 2024
Committee Members in attendance: Danielle George
Administrators: Cynthia Alexander, Jen Gulko
Guests: Erica Wallstrom,
An overview of the year-long curriculum work in science at the high school was presented to the
committee. The presentation outlined our goals as a high school science department:
developing a common definition and understanding of phenomena and phenomena-based
learning, and alignment to the Next Generation Science Standards.
The outcomes this year have focused on collective learning and growth, implementing
phenomena-based instruction, and focusing on the cross-cutting standards and science
practices. These practices will have a feedback process that is being developed.
Submitted by: Cynthia Alexander
The next meeting will be held in the fall.
17WestStreetFreeport,ME04032· 207-865-0928

“Toinspireandsupporteverylearnerbychallengingminds,buildingcharacter,sparkingcreativity,andnurturingpassions.”
JeanSkorapa,SuperintendentofSchools CynthiaAlexander,AssistantSuperintendentofSchools
KellyWentworth,DirectorofFinance&HumanResources JuneSellers,Ed.D.,DirectorofInstructionalSupport
Curriculumand ProgramDevelopment CommitteeReport
Committee: Curriculum and Program Development
Meeting date: January 23, 2024
Committee Members in attendance: Candy deCsipkes, Danielle George
Administrator: Cynthia Alexander
Guests: Susan Dee, RSU5 Literacy Strategist
Susan Dee shared a presentation of the timeline and overview of our Comprehensive Literacy
Framework in PK-5 since the fall of 2017 through present day. The presentation outlined our
revisions to the curriculum overtime and the importance of the role of coaching and ongoing
professional development in RSU5. The presentation culminated with a review of the current
reading and writing units including phonics, grammar and spelling.
Submitted by: Cynthia Alexander
The next meeting will be held on April 9, 2024 at 1:00 p.m.
17WestStreetFreeport,ME04032· 207-865-0928
